item.title: Why Staying Under 2°C Was Never Physically Plausible
item.description:
#infographic

Abstract

For the last two decades, global climate policy has revolved around a simple headline number: keep warming “well below 2 °C” and preferably 1.5 °C. That sounds like a clear plan. The awkward reality is that staying under 2 °C was never physically plausible on the trajectory we were actually on, once you look at the basic physics and the modelling assumptions underneath the glossy scenarios.

#explainer_video

This essay uses James Hansen’s recent “Colorful Chart” – a plot of greenhouse‑gas forcing over time – as a starting point. The chart is not just a picture of how much we’ve warmed; it’s a picture of how quickly we have been turning up the heating, and how that rate of change is now incompatible with the way “2 °C” was sold to the public and to policymakers [@Hansen2025Colorful].

item.title: Emsemioverse Style Guide
item.description:
Emsemioverse Style Guide

Introduction

This document is a manual of how I style my writing: construct it in a way that I think accurately and precisely communicates my ideas.

The Written Voice

This section contains a general description of the voice I try to use. By voice, I mean the mix of content and style that gives my writing its personality. I wrote every rule in this manual with the primary objective of clarifying my voice.

item.title: Implausible 2c Research & Media
item.description: This directory contains research around the idea that the 2 degrees of global warming that is used as a benchmark for most climate efforts was never feasible.

item.title: ActivityPub: Institutional Capture of Insurgent Protocol
item.description:
1. Introduction: From Insurgent Protocol to Institutional Dead Zone

In the last decade, the “fediverse” has been widely promoted as a concrete manifestation of a different kind of social web: one built on federation rather than platform enclosure, local governance rather than global terms of service, and open protocols rather than proprietary APIs. At the center of this story sits ActivityPub, a protocol standardized at the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) and frequently described as the technical backbone of decentralized social networking. Mastodon, the most visible ActivityPub-based platform, has become the public face of this alternative. From the perspective of traditional web governance, ActivityPub looks like a success story. A grassroots set of practices and experiments coalesced into a protocol; the protocol was taken up by a major standards body; a flagship implementation gained substantial public recognition; large firms, including Meta, announced plans to integrate with it. The story appears to track the standard life-cycle of web technologies: experimental beginnings, formal consolidation, and eventual mainstream adoption. Yet the trajectory of Meta’s Threads—its attempt to plug a large-scale commercial social network into ActivityPub and “join the fediverse”—complicates this narrative. Despite intensive publicity, regulatory interest in interoperability, and the symbolic weight of a major platform endorsing a W3C standard, Threads’ federation has so far failed to either (a) transform the fediverse into a profitable growth engine for Meta or (b) establish ActivityPub as a living, generative substrate for a new kind of social web. Federated support exists, but it is marginal to Threads’ core product. Many fediverse communities remain skeptical or actively hostile. The promise of a vibrant, open ecosystem lubricated by a neutral standard has not materialized. This paper starts from that tension. Why does a protocol that appears to have successfully navigated the standards pipeline—and attracted the attention of one of the world’s largest platforms—produce such an anemic result, both politically and economically? Why does a success in the language of standards bodies (a W3C Recommendation, a large deployed base, corporate adoption) coincide with a sense that something has gone dead at the heart of the “open social web” project? We argue that these outcomes are not incidental but symptomatic of a deeper structural dynamic: standardization as counterinsurgency. By this we mean a patterned institutional response in which standards bodies absorb infrastructural projects that originate as challenges to the dominant platform order, translate them into narrow technical artifacts, and stabilize them in forms that are highly legible to corporate and regulatory actors but stripped of much of their generative and political force. To develop this argument, we introduce three linked concepts:

item.title: Exploring the semantics of The Late Marx citers
item.description:
import csv, json, re, time, random
import requests

OUTFILE = "abstracts.csv"
CONTACT_EMAIL = "emsenn@emsenn.net"
MAX_RESULTS = 200
SLEEP = 1.2
SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)

QUERY_ANDERSON = '"Kevin B. Anderson" "The Late Marx and the Russian Road"'
QUERY_CONTROL  = 'Marxism OR "Karl Marx"'
MARX_FILTER = re.compile(r"(marx|marxism|historical materialism|capitalis\w*|communis\w*|class struggle)", re.I)
BAD_SUBJECTS = {"chemistry", "physics", "materials", "engineering", "medicine", "biology", "geology"}

def query_crossref(query, rows=100, cursor="*"):
    url = "https://api.crossref.org/works"
    params = {"query.bibliographic": query, "rows": rows, "cursor": cursor, "mailto": CONTACT_EMAIL}
    while True:
        r = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=30)
        if r.status_code != 200:
            print("Error", r.status_code, r.text)
            break
        data = r.json()
        items = data["message"]["items"]
        for it in items:
            yield it
        cursor_next = data["message"].get("next-cursor")
        if not cursor_next or len(items) == 0:
            break
        params["cursor"] = cursor_next
        time.sleep(SLEEP)

def clean(text):
    if not text:
        return ""
    return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", str(text)).strip()

def extract(it):
    doi = it.get("DOI", "")
    title = clean(" ".join(it.get("title", [])))
    abstract = clean(it.get("abstract", ""))
    year = None
    for fld in ("published-print","published-online","created"):
        if fld in it and "date-parts" in it[fld]:
            year = it[fld]["date-parts"][0][0]
            break
    authors = ", ".join([a.get("family","") for a in it.get("author",[]) if "family" in a])
    publisher = it.get("publisher", "")
    subjects = [s.lower() for s in it.get("subject", [])]
    lang = it.get("language","").lower() if it.get("language") else ""
    return {"doi":doi,"title":title,"abstract":abstract,"year":year,
            "authors":authors,"publisher":publisher,"subjects":subjects,"lang":lang}

def is_marxist(rec):
    """Heuristic: English + humanities/social + Marx lexicon"""
    if rec["lang"] and not rec["lang"].startswith("en"):
        return False
    if any(bad in rec["subjects"] for bad in BAD_SUBJECTS):
        return False
    text = f"{rec['title']} {rec['abstract']}".lower()
    return bool(MARX_FILTER.search(text))

# --- Main collection ---
def collect(query, label):
    records = []
    for item in query_crossref(query, rows=100):
        rec = extract(item)
        if not is_marxist(rec):
            continue
        rec["label"] = label
        records.append(rec)
        if len(records) >= MAX_RESULTS:
            break
    return records

print("Fetching Anderson-related records…")
anderson_records = collect(QUERY_ANDERSON, "cites_anderson")

print("Fetching Marxist control records…")
control_records = collect(QUERY_CONTROL, "control")

rows = anderson_records + control_records
random.shuffle(rows)

# --- Write CSV ---
with open(OUTFILE, "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=[
        "label","year","title","authors","publisher","doi","abstract"
    ])
    writer.writeheader()
    for r in rows:
        writer.writerow({k:r.get(k,"") for k in writer.fieldnames})

print(f"Wrote {len(rows)} records to {OUTFILE}")
print("Sample titles:")
for r in rows[:10]:
    print(f"- [{r['label']}] {r['year']} {r['title'][:80]}…")

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
explore-anderson-citations.py
---------------------------------
Quick analysis of whether Anderson-citing works use more 'moral' or 'method'
language, using a simple keyword frequency approach on abstracts.csv.

Input CSV must contain columns: label, text (abstract or concatenated text).
Label values: 'cites_anderson' or 'control'.

Outputs group means and 95% confidence intervals per 1000 words.
"""

import re, csv, statistics as stats
from collections import Counter

# --- Keyword patterns ---
METHOD = r"\b(method|methods|measurement|data(set)?|survey|fieldwork|interview|sampling|regress\w*|model\w*|estimate\w*|coding|reliab\w*|validi\w*|operationali\w*|variable\w*|hypothes\w*|test(ed|ing)?|significan\w*|simulat\w*|network\w*|experiment\w*|appendix|replicat\w*|code|archive|corpus|algorithm)\b"
MORAL  = r"\b(redempt\w*|inclusion|inclusive|recognit\w*|plural\w*|eurocentr\w*|decolonial\w*|coloniality|humanism|ethic(s|al)\b(?!\s*protocol)|empath\w*|conscience|guilt|atone\w*|moral\w*|legitim\w*|rehabilitat\w*|reconciliat\w*|solidarit\w*|utopian\w*|hopeful|hope|salvat\w*|justice|injustic\w*|violenc\w*|suffer\w*|oppress\w*|emancipat\w*|dignit\w*|liberat\w*|redress|repair|healing)\b"

METHOD_RE = re.compile(METHOD, re.I)
MORAL_RE  = re.compile(MORAL, re.I)

def normalize(text):
    if not text:
        return ""
    return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text).lower()

def count_keywords(text, pattern):
    return len(pattern.findall(text))

def load_records(path="abstracts.csv"):
    records = []
    with open(path, newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        for row in reader:
            label = row.get("label","").strip()
            # Concatenate title + abstract for analysis
            text = normalize((row.get("title","") or "") + " " + (row.get("abstract","") or ""))
            if not text:
                continue
            records.append({"label":label, "text":text})
    return records

def compute_scores(records):
    results = []
    for rec in records:
        words = rec["text"].split()
        n_words = len(words) if words else 1
        method_count = count_keywords(rec["text"], METHOD_RE)
        moral_count  = count_keywords(rec["text"], MORAL_RE)
        # Per 1000 words
        mth_per_1k = method_count * 1000.0 / n_words
        mor_per_1k = moral_count * 1000.0 / n_words
        results.append({
            "label": rec["label"],
            "method_per_1k": mth_per_1k,
            "moral_per_1k": mor_per_1k
        })
    return results

def group_stats(results):
    groups = {}
    for r in results:
        groups.setdefault(r["label"], {"method":[], "moral":[]})
        groups[r["label"]]["method"].append(r["method_per_1k"])
        groups[r["label"]]["moral"].append(r["moral_per_1k"])
    return groups

def summarize(vals):
    if not vals:
        return (0.0,0.0,0.0)
    mean = stats.mean(vals)
    if len(vals) > 1:
        stdev = stats.pstdev(vals)
        # 95% CI ~ mean ± 1.96 * (stdev / sqrt(n))
        se = stdev / (len(vals) ** 0.5)
        ci_low = mean - 1.96 * se
        ci_high = mean + 1.96 * se
    else:
        ci_low = ci_high = mean
    return (mean, ci_low, ci_high)

def main():
    records = load_records()
    results = compute_scores(records)
    groups = group_stats(results)

    print("Group\tType\tMean\tCI_low\tCI_high\tN")
    for label, vals in groups.items():
        for kind in ("method","moral"):
            arr = vals[kind]
            mean, lo, hi = summarize(arr)
            print(f"{label}\t{kind}\t{mean:.2f}\t{lo:.2f}\t{hi:.2f}\t{len(arr)}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

item.title: Testing Collatz stabilization with RITM
item.description:
from sim import *
  import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

  # ----- Collatz relation modulo N -----
  def collatz_step(n, N):
      x = 3 * n + 1
      while x % 2 == 0:
          x //= 2
      return x % N

  def collatz_relation(N):
      pairs = [(i, collatz_step(i, N)) for i in range(N)]
      return relation_from_pairs(N, N, pairs)

  # ----- Idempotence check -----
  def is_idempotent(R, tol=0):
      CR = C_graph(R)
      diff = count_ones(mat_xor(R, CR))
      return diff <= tol, diff

  # ----- Experiment -----
  def collatz_experiment(N=20, steps=300, verbose=True):
      R = collatz_relation(N)
      Y = [row[:] for row in R]
      S_vals, sig_vals, diffs = [], [], []
      idempotent_step = None

      for k in range(steps):
          S = action_S(R, Y)
          sig = sigma_score(R, Y)
          idem, diff = is_idempotent(R)
          S_vals.append(S)
          sig_vals.append(sig)
          diffs.append(diff)
          if verbose:
              print(f"step {k:02d}: S={S:.3f}, σ={sig:.3f}, diff={diff}")

          if idem and idempotent_step is None:
              idempotent_step = k

          # comonadic evolution
          R = iterate_modal(R, 2, Y)
          Y = C_graph(Y)

      return S_vals, sig_vals, diffs, idempotent_step

  # ----- Run & Plot -----
  if __name__ == "__main__":
      N = 20
      steps = 30
      S_vals, sig_vals, diffs, idem_step = collatz_experiment(N, steps)

      plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
      plt.plot(S_vals, label='Action S')
      plt.plot(sig_vals, label='σ coherence')
      if idem_step is not None:
          plt.axvline(idem_step, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'Idempotent at {idem_step}')
      plt.xlabel('Iteration step')
      plt.ylabel('Value')
      plt.title(f'Collatz–RITM Stabilization mod {N}')
      plt.legend()
      plt.tight_layout()
      plt.show()

      if idem_step is not None:
          print(f"\nIdempotence detected at step {idem_step} (C(R)==R).")
      else:
          print("\nNo full idempotence reached within given steps.")

  # RITM-Collatz Experiment Simulator
  # Requires: sim.py in the same directory

  from sim import *
  import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

  # --- Collatz relation construction ---
  def collatz_pairs(n:int):
      pairs = []
      for i in range(1, n):
          if i % 2 == 0:
              j = i // 2
          else:
              j = 3 * i + 1
          if j < n:
              pairs.append((i, j))
      return pairs

  # --- Simulation experiment ---
  def run_collatz_experiment(n=64, steps=20, conserve_degrees=True, verbose=True):
      Y0 = line_graph(n)
      R0 = relation_from_pairs(n, n, collatz_pairs(n))

      if verbose:
          print(f"=== RITM–Collatz experiment (n={n}) ===")
      R, Y, hist = evolve_RY_noether(R0, Y0, steps=steps, conserve_degrees=conserve_degrees, verbose=verbose)

      # collect metrics
      S_vals = [S for (_,_,S,_) in hist]
      sigma_vals = [sig for (_,_,_,sig) in hist]
      return hist, S_vals, sigma_vals

  # --- Visualization ---
  def plot_collatz_dynamics(S_vals, sigma_vals):
      fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(6,6))
      ax[0].plot(S_vals, marker='o')
      ax[0].set_title('Action S over steps (RITM energy descent)')
      ax[0].set_xlabel('Step')
      ax[0].set_ylabel('S')

      ax[1].plot(sigma_vals, marker='o', color='green')
      ax[1].set_title('σ coherence (stabilization index)')
      ax[1].set_xlabel('Step')
      ax[1].set_ylabel('σ')
      plt.tight_layout()
      plt.show()

  # --- Coalgebra extraction ---
  def stabilized_indices(R, Y):
      return defect_rows(R, Y)

  if __name__ == "__main__":
      hist, S_vals, sigma_vals = run_collatz_experiment(n=32, steps=15, conserve_degrees=False)
      plot_collatz_dynamics(S_vals, sigma_vals)

      Rf, Yf, Sf, sigf = hist[-1]
      stables = stabilized_indices(Rf, Yf)
      print(f"\nStabilized (coalgebra) indices: {stables}")
      print("Final topology Yf:")
      print(show_matrix(Yf))

  # RITM-Collatz Experiment Simulator (Stepwise Comonadic Version)
  # Requires: sim.py in the same directory

  from sim import *
  import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

  # --- Stepwise comonadic operators ---
  def box_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      n = len(A)
      C = [row[:] for row in A]
      for _ in range(steps):
          C_next = mat_bool_or(C, mat_bool_mul(C, A))
          for i in range(n):
              C_next[i][i] = 1  # ensure reflexivity
          C = C_next
      return C

  def diamond_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      return transpose(box_step(transpose(A), steps=steps))

  def C_graph_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      return box_step(diamond_step(A, steps=steps), steps=steps)

  # --- Collatz relation construction ---
  def collatz_pairs(n:int):
      pairs = []
      for i in range(1, n):
          if i % 2 == 0:
              j = i // 2
          else:
              j = 3 * i + 1
          if j < n:
              pairs.append((i, j))
      return pairs

  # --- Stepwise evolve ---
  def evolve_RY_stepwise(R, Y, steps=10, closure_step=1, verbose=True):
      hist = []
      for t in range(steps):
          CY = C_graph_step(Y, steps=closure_step)
          Rn = mat_bool_mul(R, CY)
          Yn = mat_bool_or(Y, CY)
          S = action_S(Rn, Yn)
          sig = sigma_score(Rn, Yn)
          if verbose:
              print(f"t={t:02d}  S={S:.3f}  σ={sig:.3f}")
          hist.append((Rn, Yn, S, sig))
          if t>0 and abs(hist[-1][2]-hist[-2][2])<1e-9:
              break
          R, Y = Rn, Yn
      return hist

  # --- Experiment driver ---
  def run_collatz_experiment_stepwise(n=32, steps=10, closure_step=1, verbose=True):
      Y0 = line_graph(n)
      R0 = relation_from_pairs(n, n, collatz_pairs(n))
      hist = evolve_RY_stepwise(R0, Y0, steps=steps, closure_step=closure_step, verbose=verbose)
      S_vals = [S for (_,_,S,_) in hist]
      sigma_vals = [sig for (_,_,_,sig) in hist]
      return hist, S_vals, sigma_vals

  # --- Visualization ---
  def plot_collatz_dynamics(S_vals, sigma_vals):
      fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(6,6))
      ax[0].plot(S_vals, marker='o')
      ax[0].set_title('Action S over steps (RITM energy descent)')
      ax[0].set_xlabel('Step')
      ax[0].set_ylabel('S')

      ax[1].plot(sigma_vals, marker='o', color='green')
      ax[1].set_title('σ coherence (stabilization index)')
      ax[1].set_xlabel('Step')
      ax[1].set_ylabel('σ')
      plt.tight_layout()
      plt.show()

  # --- Coalgebra extraction ---
  def stabilized_indices(R, Y):
      return defect_rows(R, Y)

  if __name__ == "__main__":
      hist, S_vals, sigma_vals = run_collatz_experiment_stepwise(n=32, steps=15, closure_step=1)
      plot_collatz_dynamics(S_vals, sigma_vals)
      Rf, Yf, Sf, sigf = hist[-1]
      print(f"\nStabilized indices: {stabilized_indices(Rf, Yf)}")

  
  from sim import *
  import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
  from collections import defaultdict

  # --- Stepwise comonadic operators ---
  def box_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      n = len(A)
      C = [row[:] for row in A]
      for _ in range(steps):
          C_next = mat_bool_or(C, mat_bool_mul(C, A))
          for i in range(n):
              C_next[i][i] = 1
          C = C_next
      return C

  def diamond_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      return transpose(box_step(transpose(A), steps=steps))

  def C_graph_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      return box_step(diamond_step(A, steps=steps), steps=steps)

  # --- Collatz relation construction ---
  def collatz_pairs(n:int):
      pairs = []
      for i in range(1, n):
          if i % 2 == 0:
              j = i // 2
          else:
              j = 3 * i + 1
          if j < n:
              pairs.append((i, j))
      return pairs

  # --- Stepwise evolution ---
  def evolve_RY_stepwise(R, Y, steps=10, closure_step=1, verbose=True):
      hist = []
      for t in range(steps):
          CY = C_graph_step(Y, steps=closure_step)
          Rn = mat_bool_mul(R, CY)
          Yn = mat_bool_or(Y, CY)
          S = action_S(Rn, Yn)
          sig = sigma_score(Rn, Yn)
          if verbose:
              print(f"t={t:02d}  S={S:.3f}  σ={sig:.3f}")
          hist.append((Rn, Yn, S, sig))
          if t>0 and abs(hist[-1][2]-hist[-2][2])<1e-9:
              break
          R, Y = Rn, Yn
      return hist

  # --- Residue-class analysis ---
  def residue_partition(n:int, mod:int) -> Dict[int, List[int]]:
      part = defaultdict(list)
      for i in range(n):
          part[i % mod].append(i)
      return part

  def residue_metrics(R: List[List[int]], Y: List[List[int]], mod:int=6):
      n = len(Y)
      classes = residue_partition(n, mod)
      res_metrics = {}
      for r, idxs in classes.items():
          if not idxs:
              continue
          subR = [[R[i][j] for j in idxs] for i in idxs]
          subY = [[Y[i][j] for j in idxs] for i in idxs]
          S = action_S(subR, subY)
          sig = sigma_score(subR, subY)
          res_metrics[r] = (S, sig)
      return res_metrics

  # --- Experiment driver ---
  def run_collatz_experiment_residues(n=32, steps=10, closure_step=1, mod:int=6, verbose=True):
      Y0 = line_graph(n)
      R0 = relation_from_pairs(n, n, collatz_pairs(n))
      hist = evolve_RY_stepwise(R0, Y0, steps=steps, closure_step=closure_step, verbose=verbose)
      per_residue = []
      for R, Y, S, sig in hist:
          res = residue_metrics(R, Y, mod=mod)
          per_residue.append(res)
      return hist, per_residue

  # --- Visualization ---
  def plot_residue_dynamics(per_residue):
      mods = sorted(list(per_residue[0].keys()))
      steps = range(len(per_residue))
      fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(7,6))
      for m in mods:
          S_vals = [res[m][0] for res in per_residue]
          σ_vals = [res[m][1] for res in per_residue]
          ax[0].plot(steps, S_vals, marker='o', label=f"mod {m}")
          ax[1].plot(steps, σ_vals, marker='o', label=f"mod {m}")
      ax[0].set_title('Action S per residue class')
      ax[0].set_xlabel('Step')
      ax[0].set_ylabel('S')
      ax[0].legend()
      ax[1].set_title('σ coherence per residue class')
      ax[1].set_xlabel('Step')
      ax[1].set_ylabel('σ')
      ax[1].legend()
      plt.tight_layout()
      plt.show()

  # --- Main ---
  if __name__ == "__main__":
      hist, per_residue = run_collatz_experiment_residues(n=36, steps=10, closure_step=1, mod=6)
      plot_residue_dynamics(per_residue)
      Rf, Yf, Sf, sigf = hist[-1]
      print("Final stabilized indices:", defect_rows(Rf, Yf))

  # RITM-Collatz Experiment Simulator with Residue-Class Analysis & Decay Rates
  # Requires: sim.py in the same directory

  from sim import *
  import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
  from collections import defaultdict
  from typing import Dict, List, Tuple

  # --- Stepwise comonadic operators ---
  def box_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      n = len(A)
      C = [row[:] for row in A]
      for _ in range(steps):
          C_next = mat_bool_or(C, mat_bool_mul(C, A))
          for i in range(n):
              C_next[i][i] = 1
          C = C_next
      return C

  def diamond_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      return transpose(box_step(transpose(A), steps=steps))

  def C_graph_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      return box_step(diamond_step(A, steps=steps), steps=steps)

  # --- Collatz relation construction ---
  def collatz_pairs(n:int):
      pairs = []
      for i in range(1, n):
          if i % 2 == 0:
              j = i // 2
          else:
              j = 3 * i + 1
          if j < n:
              pairs.append((i, j))
      return pairs

  # --- Stepwise evolution ---
  def evolve_RY_stepwise(R, Y, steps=10, closure_step=1, verbose=True):
      hist = []
      for t in range(steps):
          CY = C_graph_step(Y, steps=closure_step)
          Rn = mat_bool_mul(R, CY)
          Yn = mat_bool_or(Y, CY)
          S = action_S(Rn, Yn)
          sig = sigma_score(Rn, Yn)
          if verbose:
              print(f"t={t:02d}  S={S:.3f}  σ={sig:.3f}")
          hist.append((Rn, Yn, S, sig))
          if t>0 and abs(hist[-1][2]-hist[-2][2])<1e-9:
              break
          R, Y = Rn, Yn
      return hist

  # --- Residue-class analysis ---
  def residue_partition(n:int, mod:int) -> Dict[int, List[int]]:
      part = defaultdict(list)
      for i in range(n):
          part[i % mod].append(i)
      return part

  def residue_metrics(R: List[List[int]], Y: List[List[int]], mod:int=6):
      n = len(Y)
      classes = residue_partition(n, mod)
      res_metrics = {}
      for r, idxs in classes.items():
          if not idxs:
              continue
          subR = [[R[i][j] for j in idxs] for i in idxs]
          subY = [[Y[i][j] for j in idxs] for i in idxs]
          S = action_S(subR, subY)
          sig = sigma_score(subR, subY)
          res_metrics[r] = (S, sig)
      return res_metrics

  # Compute simple decay stats: time-to-zero S, first-step drop ratio, and area under S
  DefStats = Dict[int, Dict[str, float]]

  def residue_decay_stats(per_residue: List[Dict[int, Tuple[float, float]]]) -> DefStats:
      # per_residue[t][r] = (S, σ)
      T = len(per_residue)
      residues = sorted(per_residue[0].keys())
      stats: DefStats = {}
      for r in residues:
          S_series = [per_residue[t][r][0] for t in range(T)]
          # time to hit zero S
          t0 = None
          for t, Sv in enumerate(S_series):
              if Sv == 0:
                  t0 = t
                  break
          # first-step drop ratio (avoid division by 0)
          S0 = S_series[0]
          S1 = S_series[1] if T > 1 else S0
          drop = (S0 - S1) / S0 if S0 > 0 else 1.0
          # discrete area under curve (AUC)
          auc = sum(S_series)
          stats[r] = {"time_to_zero": float(t0 if t0 is not None else -1),
                      "first_drop_ratio": float(drop),
                      "auc_S": float(auc)}
      return stats

  # --- Experiment drivers ---
  def run_collatz_experiment_residues(n=32, steps=10, closure_step=1, mod:int=6, verbose=True):
      Y0 = line_graph(n)
      R0 = relation_from_pairs(n, n, collatz_pairs(n))
      hist = evolve_RY_stepwise(R0, Y0, steps=steps, closure_step=closure_step, verbose=verbose)
      per_residue = []
      for R, Y, S, sig in hist:
          res = residue_metrics(R, Y, mod=mod)
          per_residue.append(res)
      return hist, per_residue

  # Multiple moduli wrapper

  def run_multi_moduli(n=48, steps=10, closure_step=1, mod_list:List[int]=[4,6,8,12], verbose=False):
      results = {}
      for m in mod_list:
          if verbose:
              print(f"\n=== modulus {m} ===")
          hist, per_residue = run_collatz_experiment_residues(n=n, steps=steps, closure_step=closure_step, mod=m, verbose=verbose)
          stats = residue_decay_stats(per_residue)
          results[m] = {"hist": hist, "per_residue": per_residue, "stats": stats}
      return results

  # --- Visualization ---

  def plot_residue_dynamics(per_residue, title_prefix=""):
      mods = sorted(list(per_residue[0].keys()))
      steps = range(len(per_residue))
      fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(7,6))
      for m in mods:
          S_vals = [res[m][0] for res in per_residue]
          σ_vals = [res[m][1] for res in per_residue]
          ax[0].plot(steps, S_vals, marker='o', label=f"mod {m}")
          ax[1].plot(steps, σ_vals, marker='o', label=f"mod {m}")
      ax[0].set_title(f'{title_prefix}Action S per residue class')
      ax[0].set_xlabel('Step')
      ax[0].set_ylabel('S')
      ax[0].legend()
      ax[1].set_title(f'{title_prefix}σ coherence per residue class')
      ax[1].set_xlabel('Step')
      ax[1].set_ylabel('σ')
      ax[1].legend()
      plt.tight_layout()
      plt.show()


  def plot_multi_moduli(results):
      # results[m]["per_residue"]
      M = len(results)
      figS, axesS = plt.subplots(M, 1, figsize=(7, 3*M), sharex=True)
      figσ, axesσ = plt.subplots(M, 1, figsize=(7, 3*M), sharex=True)
      if M == 1:
          axesS = [axesS]
          axesσ = [axesσ]
      for i, m in enumerate(sorted(results.keys())):
          per_residue = results[m]["per_residue"]
          residues = sorted(per_residue[0].keys())
          steps = range(len(per_residue))
          for r in residues:
              S_vals = [res[r][0] for res in per_residue]
              σ_vals = [res[r][1] for res in per_residue]
              axesS[i].plot(steps, S_vals, marker='o', label=f"r={r}")
              axesσ[i].plot(steps, σ_vals, marker='o', label=f"r={r}")
          axesS[i].set_title(f"Action S by residues (mod {m})")
          axesS[i].set_ylabel('S')
          axesS[i].legend(ncol=min(6, len(residues)))
          axesσ[i].set_title(f"σ by residues (mod {m})")
          axesσ[i].set_ylabel('σ')
          axesσ[i].legend(ncol=min(6, len(residues)))
      axesS[-1].set_xlabel('Step')
      axesσ[-1].set_xlabel('Step')
      figS.tight_layout(); figσ.tight_layout()
      plt.show()


  def print_decay_tables(results):
      # pretty-print decay stats per modulus
      for m in sorted(results.keys()):
          stats = results[m]["stats"]
          residues = sorted(stats.keys())
          print(f"\n=== Decay stats (mod {m}) ===")
          print("res  time_to_zero  first_drop  auc_S")
          for r in residues:
              s = stats[r]
              print(f"{r:>3}  {int(s['time_to_zero']):>12}  {s['first_drop_ratio']:.3f}     {s['auc_S']:.1f}")
          # modulus-level aggregates
          ttz_vals = [stats[r]['time_to_zero'] for r in residues]
          drop_vals = [stats[r]['first_drop_ratio'] for r in residues]
          auc_vals = [stats[r]['auc_S'] for r in residues]
          avg = lambda xs: sum(xs)/len(xs) if xs else 0.0
          print(f"-- avg --  {avg(ttz_vals):>12.2f}  {avg(drop_vals):.3f}     {avg(auc_vals):.1f}")

  # --- Main ---
  if __name__ == "__main__":
      # single-modulus quick run
      hist, per_residue = run_collatz_experiment_residues(n=36, steps=10, closure_step=1, mod=6)
      plot_residue_dynamics(per_residue)

      # multi-moduli comparison
      results = run_multi_moduli(n=60, steps=10, closure_step=1, mod_list=[4,6,8,12], verbose=False)
      plot_multi_moduli(results)
      print_decay_tables(results)

      # final stabilized set (for the last modulus run only)
      last_m = sorted(results.keys())[-1]
      Rf, Yf, Sf, sigf = results[last_m]["hist"][-1]
      print("\nFinal stabilized indices (last run):", defect_rows(Rf, Yf))

  # RITM—Affine Family (Collatz-like) Modal Spectrum
  # Requires: sim.py in the same directory

  from sim import *
  import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
  from collections import defaultdict
  from typing import Dict, List, Tuple, Iterable
  import math

  # =============================================
  # Stepwise comonadic operators
  # =============================================

  def box_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      n = len(A)
      C = [row[:] for row in A]
      for _ in range(steps):
          C_next = mat_bool_or(C, mat_bool_mul(C, A))
          for i in range(n):
              C_next[i][i] = 1
          C = C_next
      return C

  def diamond_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      return transpose(box_step(transpose(A), steps=steps))

  def C_graph_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      return box_step(diamond_step(A, steps=steps), steps=steps)

  # =============================================
  # Relation constructors
  # =============================================

  def pairs_from_function(n:int, f)->List[Tuple[int,int]]:
      pairs = []
      for i in range(n):
          j = f(i)
          if 0 <= j < n:
              pairs.append((i, j))
      return pairs

  # Collatz-like family: even→ n//d  (integer division), odd→ a*n + b
  # Standard Collatz is (d=2, a=3, b=1).

  def collatz_like_pairs(n:int, d:int=2, a:int=3, b:int=1) -> List[Tuple[int,int]]:
      def f(i:int)->int:
          if i==0: return 0
          if i % 2 == 0:
              return i // d
          else:
              return a*i + b
      return pairs_from_function(n, f)

  # Pure affine map f(n)=a*n + b (no parity split)

  def affine_pairs(n:int, a:int, b:int) -> List[Tuple[int,int]]:
      return pairs_from_function(n, lambda i: a*i + b)

  # =============================================
  # Stepwise evolution
  # =============================================

  def evolve_RY_stepwise(R, Y, steps=10, closure_step=1, verbose=False):
      hist = []
      for t in range(steps):
          CY = C_graph_step(Y, steps=closure_step)
          Rn = mat_bool_mul(R, CY)
          Yn = mat_bool_or(Y, CY)
          S = action_S(Rn, Yn)
          sig = sigma_score(Rn, Yn)
          if verbose:
              print(f"t={t:02d}  S={S:.3f}  σ={sig:.3f}")
          hist.append((Rn, Yn, S, sig))
          if t>0 and abs(hist[-1][2]-hist[-2][2])<1e-9:
              break
          R, Y = Rn, Yn
      return hist

  # =============================================
  # Residue-class analysis & stats
  # =============================================

  def residue_partition(n:int, mod:int) -> Dict[int, List[int]]:
      part = defaultdict(list)
      for i in range(n):
          part[i % mod].append(i)
      return part


  def residue_metrics(R: List[List[int]], Y: List[List[int]], mod:int=6):
      n = len(Y)
      classes = residue_partition(n, mod)
      res_metrics: Dict[int, Tuple[float,float]] = {}
      for r, idxs in classes.items():
          if not idxs: continue
          subR = [[R[i][j] for j in idxs] for i in idxs]
          subY = [[Y[i][j] for j in idxs] for i in idxs]
          S = action_S(subR, subY)
          sig = sigma_score(subR, subY)
          res_metrics[r] = (S, sig)
      return res_metrics


  def residue_decay_stats(per_residue: List[Dict[int, Tuple[float, float]]]):
      T = len(per_residue)
      residues = sorted(per_residue[0].keys())
      stats = {}
      for r in residues:
          S_series = [per_residue[t][r][0] for t in range(T)]
          t0 = None
          for t, Sv in enumerate(S_series):
              if Sv == 0:
                  t0 = t
                  break
          S0 = S_series[0]
          S1 = S_series[1] if T>1 else S0
          drop = (S0 - S1) / S0 if S0>0 else 1.0
          auc = sum(S_series)
          stats[r] = {"time_to_zero": float(t0 if t0 is not None else -1),
                      "first_drop_ratio": float(drop),
                      "auc_S": float(auc)}
      # Modulus-level aggregates
      def avg(xs): return sum(xs)/len(xs) if xs else 0.0
      agg = {
          "avg_time_to_zero": avg([v["time_to_zero"] for v in stats.values()]),
          "avg_first_drop_ratio": avg([v["first_drop_ratio"] for v in stats.values()]),
          "avg_auc_S": avg([v["auc_S"] for v in stats.values()])
      }
      return stats, agg

  # =============================================
  # Experiment wrappers
  # =============================================

  def run_residue_experiment(R, n:int, steps:int, closure_step:int, mod_list:List[int]):
      Y0 = line_graph(n)
      hist = evolve_RY_stepwise(R, Y0, steps=steps, closure_step=closure_step, verbose=False)
      per_mod_residue = {}
      per_mod_agg = {}
      for m in mod_list:
          per_residue = [residue_metrics(Rt, Yt, mod=m) for (Rt, Yt, _, _) in hist]
          stats, agg = residue_decay_stats(per_residue)
          per_mod_residue[m] = per_residue
          per_mod_agg[m] = agg
      return hist, per_mod_residue, per_mod_agg

  # Sweep over a,b for collatz-like family (even→n//2, odd→a*n+b)

  def sweep_collatz_like(n:int=80, steps:int=10, closure_step:int=1, a_vals:Iterable[int]=range(2,6), b_vals:Iterable[int]=range(1,6), mod_list:List[int]=[4,6,8,12]):
      results = {}
      for a in a_vals:
          for b in b_vals:
              R = relation_from_pairs(n, n, collatz_like_pairs(n, d=2, a=a, b=b))
              hist, per_mod_residue, per_mod_agg = run_residue_experiment(R, n, steps, closure_step, mod_list)
              results[(a,b)] = {"hist": hist, "per_mod_residue": per_mod_residue, "per_mod_agg": per_mod_agg}
      return results

  # Optional: sweep pure affine (not default Collatz-like)

  def sweep_affine(n:int=80, steps:int=10, closure_step:int=1, a_vals:Iterable[int]=range(2,6), b_vals:Iterable[int]=range(1,6), mod_list:List[int]=[4,6,8,12]):
      results = {}
      for a in a_vals:
          for b in b_vals:
              R = relation_from_pairs(n, n, affine_pairs(n, a, b))
              hist, per_mod_residue, per_mod_agg = run_residue_experiment(R, n, steps, closure_step, mod_list)
              results[(a,b)] = {"hist": hist, "per_mod_residue": per_mod_residue, "per_mod_agg": per_mod_agg}
      return results

  # =============================================
  # Visualization
  # =============================================

  def heatmap_mod_spectrum(results:Dict[Tuple[int,int], dict], mod:int, metric:str="avg_auc_S", title_prefix:str="Collatz-like"):
      # Build grid for a,b
      a_vals = sorted({a for (a,_) in results.keys()})
      b_vals = sorted({b for (_,b) in results.keys()})
      Z = [[0.0 for _ in b_vals] for _ in a_vals]
      for ia, a in enumerate(a_vals):
          for ib, b in enumerate(b_vals):
              Z[ia][ib] = results[(a,b)]["per_mod_agg"][mod][metric]
      fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6,5))
      im = ax.imshow(Z, aspect='auto')
      ax.set_xticks(range(len(b_vals))); ax.set_xticklabels([str(b) for b in b_vals])
      ax.set_yticks(range(len(a_vals))); ax.set_yticklabels([str(a) for a in a_vals])
      ax.set_xlabel('b'); ax.set_ylabel('a')
      ax.set_title(f"{title_prefix}: {metric} (mod {mod})")
      plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax)
      plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()


  def print_mod_agg_table(results:Dict[Tuple[int,int], dict], mod:int):
      print(f"\n=== Modulus {mod} aggregates ===")
      print(" a  b   avg_ttz  avg_drop  avg_auc_S")
      for (a,b), rec in sorted(results.items()):
          agg = rec["per_mod_agg"][mod]
          print(f" {a:>1}  {b:>1}   {agg['avg_time_to_zero']:.2f}    {agg['avg_first_drop_ratio']:.3f}     {agg['avg_auc_S']:.2f}")

  # =============================================
  # Main: run a sweep and visualize
  # =============================================
  if __name__ == "__main__":
      n = 80
      steps = 10
      closure_step = 1
      mod_list = [4,6,8,12]

      # Sweep Collatz-like family (even→n//2, odd→a*n+b)
      results = sweep_collatz_like(n=n, steps=steps, closure_step=closure_step, a_vals=range(2,6), b_vals=range(1,6), mod_list=mod_list)

      # Show heatmaps for a couple of metrics at key moduli
      heatmap_mod_spectrum(results, mod=6, metric="avg_auc_S", title_prefix="Collatz-like")
      heatmap_mod_spectrum(results, mod=4, metric="avg_auc_S", title_prefix="Collatz-like")

      # Print compact tables at mod 6 and mod 4
      print_mod_agg_table(results, mod=6)
      print_mod_agg_table(results, mod=4)

      # (Optional) compare pure affine family by uncommenting below
      aff_results = sweep_affine(n=n, steps=steps, closure_step=closure_step, a_vals=range(2,6), b_vals=range(1,6), mod_list=mod_list)
      heatmap_mod_spectrum(aff_results, mod=6, metric="avg_auc_S", title_prefix="Affine a*n+b")
      print_mod_agg_table(aff_results, mod=6)

  # RITM—Affine Family (Collatz-like) Modal Spectrum
  # Requires: sim.py in the same directory

  from sim import *
  import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
  from collections import defaultdict
  from typing import Dict, List, Tuple, Iterable
  import math

  # =============================================
  # Stepwise comonadic operators
  # =============================================

  def box_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      n = len(A)
      C = [row[:] for row in A]
      for _ in range(steps):
          C_next = mat_bool_or(C, mat_bool_mul(C, A))
          for i in range(n):
              C_next[i][i] = 1
          C = C_next
      return C

  def diamond_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      return transpose(box_step(transpose(A), steps=steps))

  def C_graph_step(A: List[List[int]], steps:int=1) -> List[List[int]]:
      return box_step(diamond_step(A, steps=steps), steps=steps)

  # =============================================
  # Relation constructors
  # =============================================

  def pairs_from_function(n:int, f)->List[Tuple[int,int]]:
      pairs = []
      for i in range(n):
          j = f(i)
          if 0 <= j < n:
              pairs.append((i, j))
      return pairs

  # Collatz-like family: even→ n//d  (integer division), odd→ a*n + b
  # Standard Collatz is (d=2, a=3, b=1).

  def collatz_like_pairs(n:int, d:int=2, a:int=3, b:int=1) -> List[Tuple[int,int]]:
      def f(i:int)->int:
          if i==0: return 0
          if i % 2 == 0:
              return i // d
          else:
              return a*i + b
      return pairs_from_function(n, f)

  # Pure affine map f(n)=a*n + b (no parity split)

  def affine_pairs(n:int, a:int, b:int) -> List[Tuple[int,int]]:
      return pairs_from_function(n, lambda i: a*i + b)

  # =============================================
  # Stepwise evolution
  # =============================================

  def evolve_RY_stepwise(R, Y, steps=10, closure_step=1, verbose=False):
      hist = []
      for t in range(steps):
          CY = C_graph_step(Y, steps=closure_step)
          Rn = mat_bool_mul(R, CY)
          Yn = mat_bool_or(Y, CY)
          S = action_S(Rn, Yn)
          sig = sigma_score(Rn, Yn)
          if verbose:
              print(f"t={t:02d}  S={S:.3f}  σ={sig:.3f}")
          hist.append((Rn, Yn, S, sig))
          if t>0 and abs(hist[-1][2]-hist[-2][2])<1e-9:
              break
          R, Y = Rn, Yn
      return hist

  # =============================================
  # Residue-class analysis & stats
  # =============================================

  def residue_partition(n:int, mod:int) -> Dict[int, List[int]]:
      part = defaultdict(list)
      for i in range(n):
          part[i % mod].append(i)
      return part


  def residue_metrics(R: List[List[int]], Y: List[List[int]], mod:int=6):
      n = len(Y)
      classes = residue_partition(n, mod)
      res_metrics: Dict[int, Tuple[float,float]] = {}
      for r, idxs in classes.items():
          if not idxs: continue
          subR = [[R[i][j] for j in idxs] for i in idxs]
          subY = [[Y[i][j] for j in idxs] for i in idxs]
          S = action_S(subR, subY)
          sig = sigma_score(subR, subY)
          res_metrics[r] = (S, sig)
      return res_metrics


  def residue_decay_stats(per_residue: List[Dict[int, Tuple[float, float]]]):
      T = len(per_residue)
      residues = sorted(per_residue[0].keys())
      stats = {}
      for r in residues:
          S_series = [per_residue[t][r][0] for t in range(T)]
          t0 = None
          for t, Sv in enumerate(S_series):
              if Sv == 0:
                  t0 = t
                  break
          S0 = S_series[0]
          S1 = S_series[1] if T>1 else S0
          drop = (S0 - S1) / S0 if S0>0 else 1.0
          auc = sum(S_series)
          stats[r] = {"time_to_zero": float(t0 if t0 is not None else -1),
                      "first_drop_ratio": float(drop),
                      "auc_S": float(auc)}
      # Modulus-level aggregates
      def avg(xs): return sum(xs)/len(xs) if xs else 0.0
      agg = {
          "avg_time_to_zero": avg([v["time_to_zero"] for v in stats.values()]),
          "avg_first_drop_ratio": avg([v["first_drop_ratio"] for v in stats.values()]),
          "avg_auc_S": avg([v["auc_S"] for v in stats.values()])
      }
      return stats, agg

  # =============================================
  # Experiment wrappers
  # =============================================

  def run_residue_experiment(R, n:int, steps:int, closure_step:int, mod_list:List[int]):
      Y0 = line_graph(n)
      hist = evolve_RY_stepwise(R, Y0, steps=steps, closure_step=closure_step, verbose=False)
      per_mod_residue = {}
      per_mod_agg = {}
      for m in mod_list:
          per_residue = [residue_metrics(Rt, Yt, mod=m) for (Rt, Yt, _, _) in hist]
          stats, agg = residue_decay_stats(per_residue)
          per_mod_residue[m] = per_residue
          per_mod_agg[m] = agg
      return hist, per_mod_residue, per_mod_agg

  # Sweep over a,b for collatz-like family (even→n//2, odd→a*n+b)

  def sweep_collatz_like(n:int=80, steps:int=10, closure_step:int=1, a_vals:Iterable[int]=range(2,6), b_vals:Iterable[int]=range(1,6), mod_list:List[int]=[4,6,8,12]):
      results = {}
      for a in a_vals:
          for b in b_vals:
              R = relation_from_pairs(n, n, collatz_like_pairs(n, d=2, a=a, b=b))
              hist, per_mod_residue, per_mod_agg = run_residue_experiment(R, n, steps, closure_step, mod_list)
              results[(a,b)] = {"hist": hist, "per_mod_residue": per_mod_residue, "per_mod_agg": per_mod_agg}
      return results

  # Optional: sweep pure affine (not default Collatz-like)

  def sweep_affine(n:int=80, steps:int=10, closure_step:int=1, a_vals:Iterable[int]=range(2,6), b_vals:Iterable[int]=range(1,6), mod_list:List[int]=[4,6,8,12]):
      results = {}
      for a in a_vals:
          for b in b_vals:
              R = relation_from_pairs(n, n, affine_pairs(n, a, b))
              hist, per_mod_residue, per_mod_agg = run_residue_experiment(R, n, steps, closure_step, mod_list)
              results[(a,b)] = {"hist": hist, "per_mod_residue": per_mod_residue, "per_mod_agg": per_mod_agg}
      return results

  # =============================================
  # Visualization
  # =============================================

  def heatmap_mod_spectrum(results:Dict[Tuple[int,int], dict], mod:int, metric:str="avg_auc_S", title_prefix:str="Collatz-like"):
      # Build grid for a,b
      a_vals = sorted({a for (a,_) in results.keys()})
      b_vals = sorted({b for (_,b) in results.keys()})
      Z = [[0.0 for _ in b_vals] for _ in a_vals]
      for ia, a in enumerate(a_vals):
          for ib, b in enumerate(b_vals):
              Z[ia][ib] = results[(a,b)]["per_mod_agg"][mod][metric]
      fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6,5))
      im = ax.imshow(Z, aspect='auto')
      ax.set_xticks(range(len(b_vals))); ax.set_xticklabels([str(b) for b in b_vals])
      ax.set_yticks(range(len(a_vals))); ax.set_yticklabels([str(a) for a in a_vals])
      ax.set_xlabel('b'); ax.set_ylabel('a')
      ax.set_title(f"{title_prefix}: {metric} (mod {mod})")
      plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax)
      plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()


  def print_mod_agg_table(results:Dict[Tuple[int,int], dict], mod:int):
      print(f"\n=== Modulus {mod} aggregates ===")
      print(" a  b   avg_ttz  avg_drop  avg_auc_S")
      for (a,b), rec in sorted(results.items()):
          agg = rec["per_mod_agg"][mod]
          print(f" {a:>1}  {b:>1}   {agg['avg_time_to_zero']:.2f}    {agg['avg_first_drop_ratio']:.3f}     {agg['avg_auc_S']:.2f}")

  # =============================================
  # Main: run a sweep and visualize
  # =============================================
  if __name__ == "__main__":
      n = 80
      steps = 10
      closure_step = 1
      mod_list = [4,6,8,12]

      # Sweep Collatz-like family (even→n//2, odd→a*n+b)
      results = sweep_collatz_like(n=n, steps=steps, closure_step=closure_step, a_vals=range(2,6), b_vals=range(1,6), mod_list=mod_list)

      # Show heatmaps for a couple of metrics at key moduli
      heatmap_mod_spectrum(results, mod=6, metric="avg_auc_S", title_prefix="Collatz-like")
      heatmap_mod_spectrum(results, mod=4, metric="avg_auc_S", title_prefix="Collatz-like")

      # Print compact tables at mod 6 and mod 4
      print_mod_agg_table(results, mod=6)
      print_mod_agg_table(results, mod=4)

      # (Optional) compare pure affine family by uncommenting below
      # aff_results = sweep_affine(n=n, steps=steps, closure_step=closure_step, a_vals=range(2,6), b_vals=range(1,6), mod_list=mod_list)
      # heatmap_mod_spectrum(aff_results, mod=6, metric="avg_auc_S", title_prefix="Affine a*n+b")
      # print_mod_agg_table(aff_results, mod=6)


  # === Parity-commutation vs instant stabilization (formal test kit) ===
  # Utilities to test the equivalence at modulus m (default 6).

  from typing import Optional

  # Solve d * t ≡ r (mod m). Returns all solutions t in [0, m-1].
  def solve_linear_congruence(d: int, r: int, m: int):
      from math import gcd
      g = gcd(d, m)
      sols = []
      if r % g != 0:
          return sols  # no solution
      d1, r1, m1 = d // g, r // g, m // g
      # find inverse of d1 modulo m1
      # (m1 and d1 are coprime now)
      inv = pow(d1, -1, m1)
      t0 = (inv * r1) % m1
      # lift to all g solutions modulo m
      for k in range(g):
          sols.append((t0 + k * m1) % m)
      return sols

  def residue_relation_collatz_like(a:int, b:int, d:int, m:int):
      # existential lift: r -> any t with f(x) ≡ t (mod m) for some x ≡ r (mod m)
      R = zeros(m, m)
      for r in range(m):
          if r % 2 == 1:
              t = (a * r + b) % m               # odd branch is single-valued mod m
              R[r][t] = 1
          else:
              # even branch: x -> x/d, lifted: all t with d*t ≡ r (mod m)
              for t in solve_linear_congruence(d, r, m):
                  R[r][t] = 1
      return R


  def extract_block(R: List[List[int]], idxs: List[int]) -> List[List[int]]:
      return [[R[i][j] for j in idxs] for i in idxs]

  def embed_block(B: List[List[int]], idxs: List[int], m:int) -> List[List[int]]:
      O = zeros(m, m)
      for a,i in enumerate(idxs):
          for b,j in enumerate(idxs):
              O[i][j] = B[a][b]
      return O

  def bool_xor(A: List[List[int]], B: List[List[int]]) -> List[List[int]]:
      n = len(A); m = len(A[0]) if n else 0
      X = zeros(n,m)
      for i in range(n):
          for j in range(m):
              X[i][j] = A[i][j] ^ B[i][j]
      return X

  def nnz(A: List[List[int]]) -> int:
      return count_ones(A)

  def nucleus_block_step(R: List[List[int]], idxs: List[int], use_step: bool=True) -> List[List[int]]:
      m = len(R)
      sub = extract_block(R, idxs)
      closed = C_graph_step(sub, steps=1) if use_step else rtc(sub)
      return embed_block(closed, idxs, m)

  def parity_commutation_defect(R: List[List[int]], m:int, use_step: bool=True) -> int:
      E = [i for i in range(m) if i % 2 == 0]
      O = [i for i in range(m) if i % 2 == 1]
      NE = nucleus_block_step(R, E, use_step=use_step)
      NO = nucleus_block_step(R, O, use_step=use_step)
      A = nucleus_block_step(NO, E, use_step=use_step)  # N_E(N_O(R))
      B = nucleus_block_step(NE, O, use_step=use_step)  # N_O(N_E(R))
      return nnz(bool_xor(A,B))

  def stabilization_depth_on_residues(R: List[List[int]], max_steps:int=6) -> Optional[int]:
      # returns the smallest t s.t. rho_t = 0 when evolving with stepwise C
      cur = [row[:] for row in R]
      for t in range(max_steps+1):
          rho = mat_xor(cur, C_graph_step(cur, steps=1))
          if nnz(rho) == 0:
              return t
          cur = C_graph_step(cur, steps=1)
      return None

  # Driver: sweep (a,b) and build confusion matrix for m=6

  def test_equivalence_mod6(a_vals=range(2,6), b_vals=range(1,6), d:int=2, m:int=6, use_step:bool=True):
      cm = {('Δ=0','τ=0'):0, ('Δ=0','τ>0'):0, ('Δ>0','τ=0'):0, ('Δ>0','τ>0'):0}
      rows = []
      for a in a_vals:
          for b in b_vals:
              R6 = residue_relation_collatz_like(a,b,d,m)
              delta = parity_commutation_defect(R6, m, use_step=use_step)
              tau   = stabilization_depth_on_residues(R6)
              key = ( 'Δ=0' if delta==0 else 'Δ>0', 'τ=0' if tau==0 else 'τ>0' )
              cm[key] += 1
              rows.append((a,b,delta,tau))
      return cm, rows

  if __name__ == "__main__":
      print("  === Parity-commutation vs instant stabilization @ mod 6 ===")
      cm, rows = test_equivalence_mod6()
      for k in [('Δ=0','τ=0'), ('Δ=0','τ>0'), ('Δ>0','τ=0'), ('Δ>0','τ>0')]:
          print(f"{k}: {cm[k]}")
      print("  Sample rows (a,b, Δ, τ):")
      for rec in rows:
          print(rec)

item.title: Reworking GnoponEmacs as a RITM tool
item.description:
When considering GnoponEmacs as a research tool for Relational Infinity-Topos Modality, I want it to be what lets me, essentially, simulate coKleisli action on my research archive as a relational topology.

Starting at what’s immutable and unique: the :ID: property every Org-roam node has.

We’re going to start with one node, called “node” representing what a node at its most general is in this modality, and so it will have a property, :node-relations:, that works like the following:

item.title: Describing Zen Fascism as Californication
item.description:
Word Salad

  • Zen fascism
  • californication
  • self as erotic savior-slave in crisis settlerism
  • there are constitutive contradictions in liberalism
    • i.e. universalism and individualism
    • stress (i.e. crisis, Collapse) exposes them
  • this creates the Liberalism Legitimacy Crisis:
    • as these contradictions intensify, liberalism loses credibility|validity as an ideological system.
      • it can’t produce the resolutions it promises
      • so it shifts to promising regulation
      • see Foucault
  • California as projection zone: California Gold Rush, Misiones Californianas (the Patronato Real of California), psychedelic revolution, Hollywood, Esalen Institute, techno-utopianism
  • californication is a process of responding to systemic contradictions by performing personal coherence?
    • doesn’t address structural tension
    • processes it as a material to be commodified
      • aesthetic, affect
      • Brian Wilson (and the Barenaked Ladies song)
      • Valley of the Dolls
      • On the Road
    • californication as genre scripting: genre structures expectation (Lauren Berlant)
    • platformal liberalism induces californication as the dominant genre of contradiction management
      • crisis is trauma, mood, healing
      • MASH television show, Red Hot Chili Peppers, skateboarding
    • cybernetic settlerism, cybernetic subject
      • you change the world by how you route events into mood, not with your beliefs or action (which come from mood)
        • Calm app, Burning Man, lifehacking, microdosing
  • Zen
    • don’t fear Collapse: be serene
    • detachmenet
      • nonreactivity
      • Apple advertisement (1984 novel)
      • Starfleet and San Fransisco (Misiones Fransiscanas?)
      • ayahuasca entrepreneurism
      • stoicism, in late-season MASH
      • eroticization of detachment
        • crisis is desireable:
      • source of good affect, source of good life
      • crisis is the source of healing, spiritual growth, betterment of self
      • liberalism is legitimate /because/ it produces crisis
      • trauma aesthetic and aesthetic trauma
      • vagus nerve, self-regulation
  • savior-slave subject
    • californicational subject is:
      • savior
        • personally responsible for managing global crises
      • slave
        • governed by external systems (and crises)
      • Collapse as performable norm:
        • collapse is not hidden or denied, it’s:
      • narrativized (healing arc)
      • monetized (survivalism, resilience, adaptation)
        • collapse becomes sustained because it is wanted
      • this makes wanting to resolve it incommensurable
        • more structures and incentive and power-force to sustain it than end it
  • RAND Institute, Summer of Love
  • shift from Trapper to BJ: critiquing to affecting
    • therapeutic
  • Disneyland
  • Watts Rebellion
  • The Electric Kool-Aid Acid Test
  • California Über Alles
  • Reaganonimics
  • The Day AfterRodney King Uprising
  • doomscrolling, rageposting

item.title: On Californication
item.description:
I’m writing this babble because I don’t have any notes on californication and that’s a deficit, after [[id:ea55f339-24a1-49ea-adc6-8786ad86e288][Babble, [2025-09-01 Mon 10:09], Mapping the neurotic platform intellectual]].

Keyword salad

Here’s some random keywords that have to do with californication, until I can come write something proper:

item.title: Accepting the Web as a Platform, not a Place
item.description:
• History
    • first the internet
    • then web
    • commerce
    • platforms
  • internal mental image of web presence didn’t change from the internet days
    • personal website
    • platforms point back there
  • practiced denial of change
    • out-of-step with the theory
    • out-of-step with normal practices
      • even “The Internet” has become a platform (IndieWeb, ActivityPub)
    • out-of-step with my own practices
  • compare to living on a land in a territory, to living as a subject in an environment
    • different values, different modes of living
    • personal websites as living on a land
    • platform presence as subject in environment
    • different modes of governance
  • self-governing as required for both
    • but different governance technologies apply:
      • personal websites
        • permanence, backlinks, “sediment”, foundation, structure
      • platform
        • fungibility, presence, ???
    • practices of self-governance for platformized web life?

Acts Narrative

Lossless aggregation of all act documents. Ordered by dependency walk seeded at relationlessness when resolvable, then remaining acts topologically. Each section embeds the original file verbatim.

1. Being Relationless (Relationlessness)

  • Path: docs/math/acts/relationlessness.md
  • Status: release
  • Slug: relationlessness

Original

---
title: Being Relationless
act: Relationlessness
slug: relationlessness
top: null
bottom: null
summary: >-
  We begin with nothing but the absence of relation; acknowledging that
  absence fixes the empty act as our initial reference point.
layer: act
status: release
tags:
  - act
---
# Being Relationless

## Interpretation

We begin with nothing but the absence of relation; acknowledging that absence fixes the empty act as our initial reference point.

No recognitions precede this step, so the empty tree `()` is the only permissible trace.  Naming that trace *Relationlessness* therefore records, without additional assumptions, that we start from the undifferentiated void.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

{{ act_nav(page.meta) }}

2. Excluding (Exclude)

  • Path: docs/math/acts/exclude.md
  • Status: release
  • Top: Relationlessness
  • Bottom: Relationlessness
  • Slug: exclude

Original

---
title: Excluding
act: Exclude
slug: exclude
top: Relationlessness
bottom: Relationlessness
summary: >-
  Speaking about the void already carves out a mentioned interior versus an
  unmentioned exterior, so an act of exclusion is forced.
layer: act
status: release
tags:
  - act
---
# Excluding

## Interpretation

Speaking about the void already carves out a mentioned interior versus an unmentioned exterior, so an act of exclusion is forced.

The trace `(Relationlessness Relationlessness)` places the same empty act in both witness and witness-ee positions.  Directing the void back at itself expresses the relation “this is not with that,” so the composition precisely captures exclusion and earns the name *Exclude*.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

{{ act_nav(page.meta) }}

3. Articulating (Articulate)

  • Path: docs/math/acts/articulate.md
  • Status: release
  • Top: Exclude
  • Bottom: Relationlessness
  • Slug: articulate

Original

---
title: Articulating
act: Articulate
slug: articulate
top: Exclude
bottom: Relationlessness
summary: >-
  As soon as exclusion exists, the attempt to exclude the void becomes
  something we can point to; that self-referential pointing is articulation.
layer: act
status: release
tags:
  - act
---
# Articulating

## Interpretation

As soon as exclusion exists, the attempt to exclude the void becomes something we can point to; that self-referential pointing is articulation.

In `(Exclude Relationlessness)` the previously defined act *Exclude* now witnesses the empty act.  Applying exclusion to its own source reveals the unavoidable articulation that occurs whenever we describe Relationlessness, so the composition is rightly named *Articulate*.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

{{ act_nav(page.meta) }}

  Once articulation is available, Relationlessness can call it forth,
  producing the first concrete emergence from the void.
layer: act
status: plan
tags:

---
# Emerging Reflex

## Interpretation

Once articulation is available, Relationlessness can call it forth, producing the first concrete emergence from the void.

The trace `(Relationlessness Articulate)` lets the void witness the act *Articulate*.  Because *Articulate* already arose from Relationlessness, composing the two records the compelled appearance of an act directly from the void, which we record as *Emerge Reflex*.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

{{ act_nav(page.meta) }}

# First Recognizing

## Interpretation

Because emergence has occurred, we can finally speak of “the first recognition”; it arises when articulation observes that emergence.

The trace `(Articulate EmergeReflex)` places *Articulate* in the witness role and the act *Emerge Reflex* as the witnessed object.  Since both ingredients have already been earned, the composition simply records “there exists a first recognition,” and naming that composite *First Recognize* captures the observation.

# Recognizing Reflex

## Interpretation

The minimal act obtained so far is simply the record of the emergence itself, and that seed is our reflex.

The trace `(EmergeReflex)` treats the act *Emerge Reflex* as a witness in its own right.  Designating that minimal witness *Reflex* introduces no new ingredients; it merely fixes notation for the unique act forced by articulating the void.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

# Iterating Reflex

## Interpretation

Applying the reflex act to its own result should yield the same sort of act again, so reflex iteration is unavoidable.

The trace `(Reflex Reflex)` feeds the act *Reflex* back into itself.  Because *Reflex* is a valid act in both positions, this composition explicitly records “reflex can witness reflex,” thereby establishing iteration without introducing any new recognitions.

# Relating

## Interpretation

With reflex iteration available, reflex may now bear witness to the mere fact of relating, so a dedicated relation act appears.

The trace `(Reflex Reflex)` places *Reflex* in both witness and witnessed roles.  Since we just proved that reflex may observe reflex, calling that self-observation *Relate* captures the insight without requiring further construction.

# Negating Relation

## Interpretation

Any affirmation of relation also reveals the possibility of denial, so we let reflex point specifically to that denial to name “not relate.”

In `(Reflex Relate)` the act *Reflex* serves as witness for the already earned act *Relate*.  Observing *Relate* through *Reflex* isolates the complementary behaviour “refusing relation,” so we designate this composition *Not Relate*.

# Witnessing

## Interpretation

Relation and its negation coexist in tension, so we must record the act that testifies to both at once.

The trace `(NotRelate Relate)` takes the already defined act *Not Relate* as its witness and *Relate* as its object.  Because both constituents exist, this composition simultaneously cites affirmation and denial, so we name it *Witness*.

# Earning Something

## Interpretation

Testifying to relation still leaves unanswered the question “what carries the testimony?”—we point reflex at that testimony to produce “something.”

Since *Witness* is established, the composition `(Reflex Witness)` applies reflex to the testimony itself.  This self-application records that an act exists which bears the testimony, so we designate the composite as *Something*.

# Witnessing Nothing

## Interpretation

Having “something” forces us to examine what *Witness* sees when it looks directly at that something; the result is “nothing.”

In `(Witness Something)` the act *Witness* observes *Something*.  Both were already earned, so their composition captures the unresolved situation where testimony points to its own bearer, producing the recognition we call *Nothing*.

To speak of bounded structure we need the minimal recognition.  Letting *Recognition* witness *Nothing* yields ***Ground Recognition*** ([GroundRecognition](ground-recognition.md)).

This composition merely names the recognition determined by outright absence, so *Ground Recognition* follows.

# Folding Void

## Interpretation

Nothing shows that *Witness* observes *Something* in two symmetric positions; folding that symmetry back on itself produces the void.

The trace `(Nothing Nothing)` reuses *Nothing* as both witness and object.  This self-application forces the symmetry between the two appearances of *Witness* and *Something* to collapse, yielding a closed loop that we name *Void*.

title: First Naming
act: FirstName
slug: first-name

# First Naming

## Interpretation

Void contains two copies of *Nothing* but does not distinguish between them; drawing attention to one copy creates a first name.

The composition `(Void Nothing)` takes the act *Void* as witness and focuses it on *Nothing*.  Since both acts already exist, this selection singles out a definite position within the folded absence, earning the title *First Name*.

# Recognizing

## Interpretation

A self-named point lacks a partner; having the act *Void* witness the act *Self Naming* produces a two-place recognition.

In the composition `(Void SelfNaming)`, the act *Void* explicitly witnesses the act *Self Naming*.  Because both acts have already been earned, this reflection creates a complementary position, so we name the resulting two-place act *Recognize*, producing a recognition.

# Contextualizing Trace

## Interpretation

A named trace requires a record that couples the naming with the whole trace.  Forcing *Assign Trace* to witness *Complete Trace* yields the act ***Contextualize Trace*** ([ContextualizeTrace](contextualize-trace.md)).

*Assign Trace* and *Complete Trace* have been earned, so their composition records the full named trace structure: `(AssignTrace CompleteTrace)` states that the assignment encloses the entire trace, which is exactly what *Contextualize Trace* must express.

*First Name* designates the chosen recognition k; composing with joins records ν_k, so *Close Principally* follows.

# Witnessing Fixed Observation

`WitnessObservation = (RecognizeObservation EquateContexts)` cites observation
acts that justify contextual indistinguishability.  In the fixed profile we
want the witness that fixed observations suffice within the fixed context, so
we compose
[FixedObservationRecognitions](fixed-observation-recognitions.md) with
[EquateContexts](equate-contexts.md):
`(FixedObservationRecognitions EquateContexts)` earns
***FixedWitnessObservation***.

`EquateContexts` identifies when two terms are indistinguishable to all
recognised contexts, and `FixedObservationRecognitions` limits attention to
observations available in the fixed profile.  Their composition records that
contextual equivalence can be witnessed using only fixed observations.

# Transition Geometry

The fixed invariant lattice describes the order–meet–join structure of
stable states, and the observation kernel sends each state into a successor
distribution.  To observe how the kernel acts geometrically, we let the
invariant lattice witness the transition law:
`(FixedInvariantLattice TransitionLaw)` earns
***TransitionGeometry***.

[FixedInvariantLattice](fixed-invariant-lattice.md) organises fixed invariants
by meet–join structure, and [TransitionLaw](transition-law.md)
provides the relational successor step.  Their composition records how the
successor relation respects the lattice—whether transitions go up, down, or
sideways in the partial order—yielding the geometric view of the kernel
structure.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

# Forming Shuffle Geometry

A geometry is the semantic structure equipped with compatible nuclei and flow.
We already formed [FormGeometry](form-geometry.md) by letting
[StructureSemantics](structure-semantics.md) witness `CommuteFlowAndNucleus`.
To specialize this to the shuffle trace dynamics, we let the same semantic
structure witness the derived compatibility of
[ShuffleFlow](shuffle-flow.md) with [Nucleate](nucleate.md):
`(StructureSemantics CommuteShuffleFlowAndNucleus)` earns
***ShuffleGeometry***.

`StructureSemantics` packages the full algebraic behavior of recognitions,
meets, joins, implication, and nuclei.
[CommuteShuffleFlowAndNucleus](commute-shuffle-flow-and-nucleus.md) shows
that the induced flow on admissible trace-shuffle classes respects the same
nucleus.  The composition therefore records a semantic calculus whose “motion”
is given by `ShuffleFlow` and whose closure is given by `Nucleate`, yielding the
shuffle geometry.

# Tracing Steps

## Interpretation

A single reduction step exists, but we need to see its effect along the entire trace.  Hence *Complete Trace* witnesses *Reduce*, producing ***Step Trace*** ([StepTrace](step-trace.md)).

The composition records the propagation of reduction steps through the trace, so *Step Trace* is earned.

109. Collapsing Trace Shuffle (CollapseShuffle)

  • Path: docs/math/acts/collapse-shuffle.md
  • Status: plan
  • Top: ShuffleTrace
  • Bottom: Nucleate
  • Slug: collapse-shuffle

Original

---
title: Collapsing Trace Shuffle
act: CollapseShuffle
slug: collapse-shuffle
top: ShuffleTrace
bottom: Nucleate
summary: Admissible shuffle reorderings leave a trace unchanged up to the nucleus, so composing `ShuffleTrace` with `Nucleate` records that such permutations are identified in the nucleus closure.
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Collapsing Trace Shuffle

[Nucleate](nucleate.md) collapses stabilized recognitions that differ only by
eventual equivalence, but no recognition yet states that shuffle-reordered
traces are eventually equivalent.  We therefore let the shuffle act witness the
nucleus closure: `(ShuffleTrace Nucleate)` earns ***CollapseShuffle***.

By construction, [ShuffleTrace](shuffle-trace.md) enumerates the admissible
reorders of progress steps that do not alter the underlying trace shape.
`Nucleate` collapses recognitions that differ only by stabilized equivalence.
Since admissible reorderings leave head/tail structure and iterative progression
intact, the composite `(ShuffleTrace Nucleate)` records that all such
reorderings lie in the kernel of the nucleus.  Nucleate therefore identifies
precisely those recognitions differing only by free shuffle of progress steps.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

{{ act_nav(page.meta) }}

# Observation Kernel

A kernel couples transition structure with observable outcomes.
[TransitionLaw](transition-law.md) provides the transition step, and
[FixedObservationRecognitions](fixed-observation-recognitions.md) restricts
observations to fixed recognitions.  Letting the observation act witness the
transition law,
`(FixedObservationRecognitions TransitionLaw)`, earns
***ObservationKernel***.

`TransitionLaw` determines how fixed states map to successor recognitions, and
`FixedObservationRecognitions` selects observables available in the fixed
profile.  Their composition assigns to each fixed invariant state the observable
distribution induced by one flow–measure step, yielding the profile’s kernel.

# Fixed Monad

Together, the nucleus functor, unit, and multiplication form a monad on the
fixed profile, so we package these laws in one act:
`(FixedMonadUnit FixedMonadMultiply)` earns ***FixedMonad***.

The usual monad laws (associativity, left/right unit) follow from the
idempotence and compatibility already earned for
[FixedNucleateProfile](fixed-nucleate-profile.md) (e.g.
`AbsorbClosure`, `EqualizeNucleusDistribution`).  `FixedMonadUnit` and
`FixedMonadMultiply` are the structural maps, and `FixedMonad` names the
resulting closure monad.

- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Context Dynamics

To capture the full evolution–collapse behaviour for contexts, we mirror the
profile kernel construction but restrict it to contexts by composing the context
nucleus with the context flow:
`(ContextNucleus ContextFlow)` earns
***ContextDynamics***.

Flow moves contexts forward, nucleus collapses them, and their composition gives
a stable profile dynamics on contexts.

# Structuring Fixed Invariant Lattice

`ProfileInvariantLattice = (StructureSemantics ProfileInvariantOrder)` packages
the lattice structure on profile invariants.  Restricting it along
[FixedInvariantOrder](fixed-invariant-order.md) yields the lattice structure
on fixed invariants:
`(ProfileInvariantLattice FixedInvariantOrder)` earns
***FixedInvariantLattice***.

`ProfileInvariantLattice` encapsulates the algebraic laws of meets, joins, and
implication on the restricted order, and `FixedInvariantOrder` narrows that
order to the fixed invariants.  Their composition records that the fixed
invariant states inherit the same lattice structure.

131. Shaping Fixed Shape (FixedShape)

  • Path: docs/math/acts/fixed-shape.md
  • Status: plan
  • Top: EnsureFixedPoint
  • Bottom: ShuffleIndexImplication
  • Slug: fixed-shape

Original

---
title: Shaping Fixed Shape
act: FixedShape
slug: fixed-shape
top: EnsureFixedPoint
bottom: ShuffleIndexImplication
summary: Apply the fixed-point construction to shuffle-indexed implication to obtain recognitions whose implication behaviour is invariant under repeated flow steps, capturing the intrinsic fixed shape.
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Shaping Fixed Shape

[ShuffleIndexImplication](shuffle-index-implication.md) describes how
recognitions transform when their premises are pushed one shuffle step
forward.  [EnsureFixedPoint](ensure-fixed-point.md) records that persistent
iterations of a flow yield fixed points.  To isolate the fixed shape, we apply
the fixed-point construction to the shuffle-indexed implication, obtaining
the recognitions whose implication behaviour does not change under repeated
shuffle stepping:
`(EnsureFixedPoint ShuffleIndexImplication)` earns
***FixedShape***.

`EnsureFixedPoint = (FixFlow IterativePersistence)` says that if iterating a
flow stabilizes along the trace, the stabilized recognitions form a fixed set
for that flow.  `ShuffleIndexImplication` is a specific flow-like act: it
moves implication structure forward one shuffle step before comparing
recognitions.  Composing them applies the fixed-point construction to that
indexed implication dynamics, yielding exactly those recognitions whose
implication behaviour is invariant under arbitrarily many shuffle steps—the
fixed shape induced by the relational dynamics.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

{{ act_nav(page.meta) }}

# Collapsing Context

Contexts collapse under the nucleus.  We restrict the profile nucleus to
contexts by letting it witness [RecognizeContext](recognize-context.md):
`(NucleateProfile RecognizeContext)` earns
***ContextNucleus***.

[NucleateProfile](nucleate-profile.md) closes recognitions under the nucleus,
and `RecognizeContext` packages context recognitions, so their composition
collapses contexts according to the profile closure.

# Fixed Physics Profile

`PhysicsProfile = (FlowObservation FlowProfile)` lifts a profile’s flow to its
physical semantics by coupling dynamics with observation.  In the fixed profile
we want observations of the fixed flow, so we let
[FlowObservation](flow-observation.md) witness
[FixedFlowProfile](fixed-flow-profile.md):
`(FlowObservation FixedFlowProfile)` earns
***FixedPhysicsProfile***.

`FlowObservation` records how observations traverse evaluation contexts under a
given flow, and `FixedFlowProfile` is the shuffle-derived flow restricted to
the fixed recognitions.  The composition couples observation traces to this flow,
yielding a physical semantics in which states evolve according to the shuffle
trace dynamics while remaining inside the fixed profile.

summary: Apply the fixed-point construction to the orbit step to obtain the stabilized layer of each orbit.
tags:
- act

---
# Fixing Orbit Layer

Orbits may stabilize: further reach steps no longer change the reachable set.
[EnsureFixedPoint](ensure-fixed-point.md) captures fixed points of flows when
iteration persists.  To obtain the stabilized layer of each orbit, we apply it
to [OrbitStep](orbit-step.md):
`(EnsureFixedPoint OrbitStep)` earns ***OrbitFixLayer***.

`OrbitStep` describes how reachable states progress from one layer to the next.
`IterativePersistence` and `EnsureFixedPoint` ensure that when repeated
stepping along the trace stabilizes, the stabilized states form a fixed set.
Their composition therefore yields, for each orbit, a stabilized layer that
further stepping does not change.

164. Flowing Shuffle-Indexed Implication (ShuffleIndexImplication)

  • Path: docs/math/acts/shuffle-index-implication.md

bottom: ImplyRecognition
summary: Compose the shuffle flow with internal implication to obtain implications indexed by one shuffle step.
tags:
- act

status: plan
---
# Flowing Shuffle-Indexed Implication

[ImplyRecognition](imply-recognition.md) records internal implication between
recognitions, and `FlowIndexImplication = (Flow ImplyRecognition)` indexed that
implication along the global flow.  Now that [ShuffleFlow](shuffle-flow.md)
is earned as the flow on admissible trace-shuffle classes, we want the
corresponding indexed implication: “if a recognition holds after one shuffle
step, what must hold now?”  Letting the shuffle flow witness
`ImplyRecognition` produces the desired act:
`(ShuffleFlow ImplyRecognition)` earns
***ShuffleIndexImplication***.

`ShuffleFlow` evolves admissible trace-shuffle families through evaluation
contexts while `ImplyRecognition` transforms recognitions via internal
implication.  The composition therefore records implications whose premises are
transported one shuffle step forward before being compared.  Naming this
composite packages the operator witnessing how recognitions transform under the
shuffle trace dynamics.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

# Transition Law

A transition law records how each invariant state spreads into its possible
successors.  With the one-step update
([FlowMeasureStep](flow-measure-step.md)) and the domain of fixed invariant
states ([FixedInvariant](fixed-invariant.md)), we let the step witness
the invariant recognitions to obtain the transition map:
`(FlowMeasureStep FixedInvariant)` earns ***TransitionLaw***.

`FixedInvariant` lists the fixed states, and `FlowMeasureStep`
describes how a step transforms any such state.  Their composition maps
each fixed invariant into its evolution–measurement successors, yielding the
transition law of the observation kernel.

# Fixed Monad Multiplication

Applying the nucleus twice should collapse as a single closure.  The monad
multiplication identifies `N(N(X))` with `N(X)`, so we let the nucleus observe
its own action:
`(FixedNucleateProfile FixedNucleateProfile)` earns
***FixedMonadMultiply***.

`FixedNucleateProfile` already idempotently closes recognitions, and the
composition records the multiplication `μ_X : N(N(X)) → N(X)` implementing this
idempotence.
