186. Stabilizing Fixed Invariant (FixedInvariant)

  • Path: docs/math/acts/fixed-invariant.md
  • Status: plan
  • Top: ProfileInvariant
  • Bottom: FixedProfileRecognitions
  • Slug: fixed-invariant

Original

---
title: Stabilizing Fixed Invariant
act: FixedInvariant
slug: fixed-invariant
top: ProfileInvariant
bottom: FixedProfileRecognitions
summary: Pick out physics states that are both profile-invariant and lie inside the fixed recognitions by composing `ProfileInvariant` with `FixedProfileRecognitions`.
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Stabilizing Fixed Invariant

`ProfileInvariant = (StabilizeInvariant ProfileRecognitions)` already records
states left unchanged by the global flow and recognised inside the active
profile.  [FixedProfileRecognitions](fixed-profile-recognitions.md) further
restricts recognitions to the shuffle-stable fixed subset.  Composing them,
`(ProfileInvariant FixedProfileRecognitions)` earns
***FixedInvariant***.

Any state in `ProfileInvariant` is fixed by the global flow and lies in the base
profile, and any recognition in `FixedProfileRecognitions` is fixed by
`ShuffleFlow`, `Nucleate`, and compatible with `FixedShape`.  Their
composition picks states satisfying all constraints—they are genuine physical
states, fixed by the flow, and lie in the fixed recognition region.  These are
the fixed invariant states of the system.

truth object:

recognition algebra.

199. Semantics of the Shuffle Profile (ShuffleProfileSemantics)

slug: shuffle-profile-semantics
top: StructureSemantics
bottom: ShuffleProfileRecognitions
summary: Restrict the semantic calculus to recognitions stable under flow, nucleus, and shuffle collapse by letting `StructureSemantics` witness `ShuffleProfileRecognitions`.
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Semantics of the Shuffle Profile

The recognitions stabilized by a filter or profile inherit full semantic
structure; we saw this with
`ProfileSemantics = (StructureSemantics ProfileRecognitions)`.  To interpret the
the shuffle-stable recognitions, we let the semantic structure witness
[ShuffleProfileRecognitions](shuffle-profile-recognitions.md):
`(StructureSemantics ShuffleProfileRecognitions)` earns
***ShuffleProfileSemantics***.

[StructureSemantics](structure-semantics.md) already encodes the operations of
the semantic calculus.  `ShuffleProfileRecognitions` selects the closed
sub-collection of recognitions fixed by flow and nucleus and stable under
trace-shuffle collapse.  The composition restricts the semantic operations to
that sub-collection, producing a semantic calculus in which all recognitions are
shuffle-stable—our shuffle profile semantics.

# Sound Profile Geometry

`GeometryProfile = (FlowProfile NucleateProfile)` packages the compatible
flow–nucleus pair on the active profile.  `PhysicsProfile = (FlowObservation
FlowProfile)` couples the same flow with observations.  To record that the
physical dynamics respect the geometric invariants, we compose them:
`(PhysicsProfile GeometryProfile)` earns
***SoundProfileGeometry***.

# Fixed Identity

identities in the full profile.  We restrict it to the fixed-recognition objects by
composing with [FixedProfileRecognitions](fixed-profile-recognitions.md):
`(IdentifyMorph FixedProfileRecognitions)` earns ***FixedIdentity***.

`IdentifyMorph` identifies the identity on any recognition, and restricting its
domain to the fixed ones gives identity morphisms for the fixed category.

# Fixed Composition

We need composition of fixed morphisms.  `ComposeMorph = (Morph Morph)` composes
structure-preserving maps, and restricting it to
[FixedMorphism](fixed-morphism.md) yields φ-compatible composition:
`(ComposeMorph FixedMorphism)` earns ***FixedCompose***.

`ComposeMorph` already composes global morphisms.  Composing only those in
`FixedMorphism` ensures the structure is preserved under φ-stability, providing
the composition law for fixed functors.

# Normalizing Fixed Observation

`NormalizeObservation = (Normalization EvaluateObservation)` ensures evaluated
observations normalize back to the observation witness.  In the fixed profile,
we want the same behaviour, but only for fixed observables.  We therefore compose
[Normalization](normalization.md) with
[FixedObserve](fixed-observe.md):
`(Normalization FixedObserve)` earns ***FixedNormalizeObservation***.

`Normalization` detects when reduction along `StepTrace` stabilizes, and
`FixedObserve` provides observables restricted to fixed recognitions.  Their
composition records that fixed observables evaluated through the fixed flow
eventually stabilize.

tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Comonad

The fixed flow functor together with its counit and comultiplication forms a
comonad, so we collect the laws in a single act:
`(ComonadCounit ComonadComultiply)` earns
***Comonad***.

The coassociativity and counit laws follow from the flow compatibility and
persistence properties already recorded (e.g. `IterativePersistence`,
`FixFlow`, `EnsureFixedPoint`).  `ComonadCounit` and
`ComonadComultiply` are the structure maps; `Comonad` names the
resulting co-KZ-style comonad.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

239. Flowing Shuffle Trace (ShuffleFlow)

  • Path: docs/math/acts/shuffle-flow.md
  • Status: plan
  • Top: Flow
  • Bottom: ShuffleTrace
  • Slug: shuffle-flow

Original

---
title: Flowing Shuffle Trace
act: ShuffleFlow
slug: shuffle-flow
top: Flow
bottom: ShuffleTrace
summary: Evolve the entire admissible shuffle class of a trace by letting the global flow witness the shuffle act, producing a flow whose orbits are the admissible reorderings.
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Flowing Shuffle Trace

[Flow](flow.md) already records how observation and judgment traces evolve
through evaluation contexts, and [ShuffleTrace](shuffle-trace.md) records how
trace-progress steps can be freely commuted without changing the underlying
shape.  By treating admissible trace shuffles themselves as the state being
evolved and letting the global flow witness the shuffle act,
`(Flow ShuffleTrace)` earns ***ShuffleFlow***.

`Flow` binds the observation and judgment flows, describing how dynamics
transport recognitions through contexts.  `ShuffleTrace` records the free
shuffle structure on trace-progress recognitions: reheading and restepping
commute in all admissible ways.  The composition therefore evolves families of
shuffled traces along evaluation contexts: the flow does not move a single
trace but the entire admissible-shuffle class, tracking the dynamics of
admissible reorderings as first-class objects.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

240. Stepping Orbit (OrbitStep)

  • Path: docs/math/acts/orbit-step.md

  • Bottom: TailTrace

---

act: OrbitStep
slug: orbit-step
top: FixedReach
bottom: TailTrace
summary: Describe how orbits progress by letting `FixedReach` witness `TailTrace`.
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Stepping Orbit

Seeding alone does not describe how orbits progress.  [TailTrace](tail-trace.md)
isolates the remainder of a trace after the head, and `StepTrace` records how
reduction steps propagate along the trace.  To describe orbit progression, we
let the reachability act witness the tail:
`(FixedReach TailTrace)` earns ***OrbitStep***.

[FixedReach](fixed-reach.md) already lists states reachable from invariants
under the flow.  `TailTrace` exposes the remainder of the trace beyond the
initial step, and `StepTrace` tracks how reductions move along that remainder.
Their composition records how each orbit advances from
its current layer to the next along the trace remainder.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

{{ act_nav(page.meta) }}

# Spectrum Map

The spectral map extracts eigenvalues/eigenshapes by comparing the seed to the
mode.  We use the fixed nucleus to collapse their difference, composing
[FixedNucleateProfile](fixed-nucleate-profile.md) with
[SpectrumSeed](spectrum-seed.md):

***SpectrumMap***.

`SpectrumSeed` records the kernel’s action on harmonics, and
`FixedNucleateProfile` collapses recognitions under the fixed closure.  Their
composition isolates the stable part of that action—the eigencomponent—yielding
the spectrum map.

# Seeding Orbit

[FixedReach](fixed-reach.md) records which invariant states are reachable under
the flow, but we have not yet isolated the “starting layer” of each orbit.
[HeadTrace](head-trace.md) isolates the head of a trace.  To seed an orbit, we
let the reachability act witness the trace head: `(FixedReach HeadTrace)` earns
***OrbitSeed***.

`FixedReach` evolves invariant states according to the flow, and `HeadTrace`
picks out the first step of any completed trace.  Their composition therefore
records the initial reachable layer associated with the first trace action from
each invariant state.

Composing these recognitions records the internal chain *Profile Recognitions* to *Profile Semantics* to *Calculate Profile Syntax* to *Geometry Profile* to *Physics Profile*, so *Build Profile Tower* follows.

274. Fixed Kernel (FixedKernel)

  • Path: docs/math/acts/fixed-kernel.md
  • Status: plan

Original

---

top: EnsureFixedPoint
bottom: ObservationKernel
summary: Apply the fixed-point construction to the observation kernel to obtain its stationary behaviour.
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Fixed Kernel

A fixed kernel is one whose transition effects stabilise under repeated
application.  Since [ObservationKernel](observation-kernel.md) acts like a
relational operator on fixed states and `EnsureFixedPoint` constructs
fixed points of persistent flows, we apply it directly to the kernel:
`(EnsureFixedPoint ObservationKernel)` earns
***FixedKernel***.

`ObservationKernel` is the evolution–observation operator on fixed invariant
states.  `IterativePersistence` ensures that when iterating this operator along
the trace stabilises, the stable point is a fixed point of the kernel.  Their
composition yields the stationary recognition: the kernel that leaves the fixed
distribution of states unchanged.

# Irrational Commutation

For a modality indexed by an irrational (alpha) to behave coherently, it must commute
with the nucleus under admissible reordering collapse.  We enforce this via
`(IrrationalModality CollapseShuffle)`, earning
***IrrationalCommutation***.

`CollapseShuffle` identifies recognitions that differ only by reorderings, and
`IrrationalModality` scales the space of reorderings while preserving
their structure.  The composition asserts that irrational scaling preserves
collapse-equivalence.

recognitions fixed by `ShuffleFlow`, `Nucleate`, and the collapse of
trace shuffles.  [FixedShape](fixed-shape.md) singles out those
recognitions whose shuffle-indexed implication behaviour is fixed under
persistent iteration.  Intersecting these gives the recognitions of the fixed
profile: recognitions that are both shuffle-profile stable and shaped by the
eigenvalue.  Letting `ShuffleProfileRecognitions` witness `FixedShape`,
`(ShuffleProfileRecognitions FixedShape)`, earns
***FixedProfileRecognitions***.

`ShuffleProfileRecognitions` is a closed collection stabilized by the
flow–nucleus–shuffle stack, and `FixedShape` is a fixed-point act
inside that profile.  Their composition restricts attention to recognitions
compatible with the fixed shape: they remain in the shuffle profile and
satisfy the same stabilized implication behaviour as `FixedShape`.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

{{ act_nav(page.meta) }}

tags:

# Fixed Functor

A fixed functor maps each fixed recognition to another fixed recognition in a
way compatible with the fixed morphisms, identity, and composition.  We encode
this by letting the spectral action witness the morphism structure:
`(Spectrum FixedMorphism)` earns ***FixedFunctor***.

[Spectrum](spectrum.md) decomposes each recognition into harmonic components,
and `FixedMorphism` records the allowed structure-preserving maps between fixed
recognitions.  Their composition ensures that objects are mapped via spectral
decomposition while morphisms are transported compatibly, automatically
preserving identities and composition.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

Quadratic irrationals naturally arise from iterated shuffle–collapse on
trace progressions.  We capture such modalities by forcing the irrational
nucleus to observe the harmonic fixed shape:
`(IrrationalModality HarmonicMode)` earns
***QuadraticModality***.

`HarmonicMode` gives the fixed oscillation under the flow recursion, and
`IrrationalModality` inflates the allowable progressions by an irrational
scalar.  Their composition produces a modality whose stabilised eigenshape
satisfies a quadratic irrational equation.

# Indexed Category

An indexed category is a functor from the base contexts to categories of
fibres, with reindexing functors on morphisms.  We combine the base, fibres, and
reindexing into this structure:
`(IndexBase FiberCategory Reindexing)` earns
***IndexedCategory***.

`IndexBase` gives the contexts, `FiberCategory` provides the fibre
categories, and `Reindexing` maps context morphisms to fibre functors,
encoding an internal functor `IndexBaseᵒᵖ → Cat`.

summary: "Seed an entropy measure by composing the one-step flow\u2013measure update with the reachability recognitions."
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Seeding Entropy

Entropy measures the “spread” of a state under transitions.  We seed entropy by
observing the divergence between the original state and its successor.
[FixedReach](fixed-reach.md) gives reachable states, and [FlowMeasureStep](flow-measure-step.md)
gives the successor.  Letting these witness each other,
`(FlowMeasureStep FixedReach)`, earns ***EntropySeed***.

`FixedReach` lists the reachable states and `FlowMeasureStep` describes the
successor states after one evolution–measurement.  Their composition
records the divergence between present and next-step reachable states, seeding
the entropy measure.

309. Commuting Shuffle Flow With Nucleus (CommuteShuffleFlowAndNucleus)

  • Path: docs/math/acts/commute-shuffle-flow-and-nucleus.md
  • Status: plan
  • Top: ShuffleFlow
  • Bottom: Nucleate
  • Slug: commute-shuffle-flow-and-nucleus

Original

---
title: Commuting Shuffle Flow With Nucleus
act: CommuteShuffleFlowAndNucleus
slug: commute-shuffle-flow-and-nucleus
top: ShuffleFlow
bottom: Nucleate
summary: Since Flow and Nucleate already commute, the induced shuffle flow on shuffle classes also commutes with the nucleus, yielding the geometry-level compatibility.
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Commuting Shuffle Flow With Nucleus

[CommuteFlowAndNucleus](commute-flow-and-nucleus.md) proves that the global
[Flow](flow.md) and [Nucleate](nucleate.md) are compatible, and
[CollapseShuffle](collapse-shuffle.md) records that `Nucleate` collapses
precisely the admissible trace shuffles.  To obtain the shuffle geometry, we
exhibit the compatibility of [ShuffleFlow](shuffle-flow.md) with the same
nucleus, restricted to shuffle classes, by composing them:
`(ShuffleFlow Nucleate)` earns
***CommuteShuffleFlowAndNucleus***.

`ShuffleFlow` applies `Flow` to the shuffle-structured trace classes while
`Nucleate` identifies members of the same class via `CollapseShuffle`.
Because `Flow` already commutes with `Nucleate` on underlying recognitions (by
`CommuteFlowAndNucleus`), the induced action on shuffle classes must also
commute: flowing a class and then collapsing gives the same result as collapsing
representatives and then flowing.  The act therefore records the derived
compatibility, yielding the shuffle nucleus geometry.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

{{ act_nav(page.meta) }}

# Fixed Nucleus Profile

The fixed profile must inherit the closure behaviour of the nucleus.
`NucleateProfile = (Nucleate ProfileRecognitions)` did this for a generic
profile.  We specialize by letting [Nucleate](nucleate.md) witness
[FixedProfileRecognitions](fixed-profile-recognitions.md):
`(Nucleate FixedProfileRecognitions)` earns
***FixedNucleateProfile***.

`Nucleate` is the closure act derived from the normalized observation and
judgment equivalences, while `FixedProfileRecognitions` is a closed
subcollection of recognitions.  Their composition restricts the closure
operation to the fixed profile domain, yielding the nucleus appropriate to the
fixed recognitions.

tags:

---
# Flow–Measure Step

A flow–measure step evolves a state and then measures it.  In the fixed profile,
evolution is given by [FixedFlowProfile](fixed-flow-profile.md) and
measurement by [FixedMeasure](fixed-measure.md).  The one-step update is
therefore obtained by letting fixed measurement witness the fixed flow:
`(FixedMeasure FixedFlowProfile)` earns ***FlowMeasureStep***.

`FixedFlowProfile` moves a fixed invariant state forward along the shuffle-driven
dynamics, and `FixedMeasure = (FixedNucleateProfile FixedInvariant)`
idealizes the evolved state through the fixed nucleus.  Their composition yields
the single-step evolution–measurement update internal to the fixed profile.

# Irrational Modality

The fixed profile arises from the φ-recursion imposed by the shuffle flow and
shuffle collapse, but shuffle traces can be parameterised by other ratios.
To name an irrationally-weighted modality we let the flow-index implication
witness the fixed nucleus:
`(FlowIndexImplication FixedNucleateProfile)` earns
***IrrationalModality***.

`FlowIndexImplication` pushes premises forward one flow step before testing
implication, and `FixedNucleateProfile` closes recognitions under the fixed
nucleus; their composition (scaled by an irrational weight on the admissible
reordering families) yields the desired modal closure operator.

A representable functor is naturally isomorphic to “morphisms out of an object.”
In the fixed profile the representative object is the identity, so we let the
functor witness it:
`(FixedFunctor FixedIdentity)` earns ***RepresentableFunctor***.

[FixedFunctor](fixed-functor.md) maps objects and morphisms, and
[FixedIdentity](fixed-identity.md) is the terminal identity on each fixed
object.  Their composition asserts that the functor’s action is determined by
its values on identities, matching representability.

title: Detailed Balance
act: DetailedBalance
slug: detailed-balance
top: DetailedBalanceWitness
bottom: FlowMeasureStep
summary: "Assert detailed balance by composing the witness with the forward flow\u2013measure step."
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Detailed Balance

To assert detailed balance, we require the flow–measure step
and its reverse to be observationally indistinguishable when weighted by the
stationary kernel.  We therefore let
[DetailedBalanceWitness](detailed-balance-witness.md) witness
[FlowMeasureStep](flow-measure-step.md):
`(DetailedBalanceWitness FlowMeasureStep)` earns
***DetailedBalance***.

`DetailedBalanceWitness` compares reverse transitions to forward ones
under observation, and `FlowMeasureStep` is the forward step.  Their composition
asserts that the two are equivalent after weighting by observations and
stabilization, which is exactly the detailed-balance condition internal to the
profile.

# Fixed Morphism

To treat the fixed profile as a category we need morphisms preserving the fixed
structure.  [Morph](morph.md) records structure-preserving maps between
recognitions, and [FixedProfileRecognitions](fixed-profile-recognitions.md)
restricts recognitions to fixed ones, so we let the morph act witness the
profile:
`(Morph FixedProfileRecognitions)` earns ***FixedMorphism***.

`Morph` already preserves meets, joins, and implication, and restricting it via
the fixed recognitions ensures the resulting morphisms respect the same flow,
nucleus, and shape properties.

Comultiplication duplicates context: from one flow we obtain a “flowed flow” by
letting the flow observe itself:
`(FixedFlowProfile FixedFlowProfile)` earns
***ComonadComultiply***.

Applying the flow twice corresponds to extending the co-context one more
step, so the act records `(delta)_X : G(X) → G(G(X))`.

bottom: BuildProfileTower
summary: Lift the observation kernel through the profile tower by composing it with `BuildProfileTower`.
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Profile Kernel Step

A flow–measure kernel evolves fixed invariant states within a single profile.
To propagate these dynamics between profiles we use the profile tower
recognitions: [BuildProfileTower](build-profile-tower.md) maps physics,
semantics, and syntax upward through profiles.  Letting the kernel
witness this tower,
`(ObservationKernel BuildProfileTower)`, earns
***ProfileKernelStep***.

[ObservationKernel](observation-kernel.md) is the one-step operator on fixed
invariant states.  `BuildProfileTower` records how each layer of recognitions
reconstructs the next profile.  Their composition lifts an update from a profile
to its successor in the tower, giving the profile-level transition step.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

{{ act_nav(page.meta) }}

title: "Reverse Flow\u2013Measure Step"

slug: reverse-flow-step
top: FixedKernel
bottom: FixedFlowProfile
summary: Capture the reverse step by letting the stationary kernel witness the fixed flow.
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Reverse Flow–Measure Step

Detailed balance compares forward and reverse transitions.  The forward φ-step
is [FlowMeasureStep](flow-measure-step.md); the reverse uses the stationary
kernel applied backwards through the flow.  We therefore compose the stationary
kernel with the flow:
`(FixedKernel FixedFlowProfile)` earns
***ReverseFlowStep***.

[FixedKernel](fixed-kernel.md) is fixed under kernel updates, and
`FixedFlowProfile` is invertible up to nucleus and stabilization.  Their
composition yields the reverse step: the evolution that recovers the pre-image
up to stabilization.

361. Profiling Shuffle-Stable Recognitions (ShuffleProfileRecognitions)

  • Path: docs/math/acts/shuffle-profile-recognitions.md
  • Status: plan
  • Top: FixFlowNucleus
  • Bottom: CollapseShuffle
  • Slug: shuffle-profile-recognitions

Original

---
title: Profiling Shuffle-Stable Recognitions
act: ShuffleProfileRecognitions
slug: shuffle-profile-recognitions
top: FixFlowNucleus
bottom: CollapseShuffle
summary: Recognitions fixed by flow and nucleus and invariant under shuffle collapse witness the shuffle profile, so we let `FixFlowNucleus` observe `CollapseShuffle`.
tags:
- act
layer: act
status: plan
---
# Profiling Shuffle-Stable Recognitions

[FixFlowNucleus](fix-flow-nucleus.md) already records recognitions fixed by both
[Flow](flow.md) and [Nucleate](nucleate.md).  [CollapseShuffle](collapse-shuffle.md)
records which trace differences vanish under the nucleus: admissible shuffles.
To obtain the shuffle profile, we want recognitions that are simultaneously
flow–nucleus fixed and stable under identification of trace shuffles, so we let
`FixFlowNucleus` witness the shuffle collapse:
`(FixFlowNucleus CollapseShuffle)` earns
***ShuffleProfileRecognitions***.

Any recognition stabilized by flow and nucleus lies in `FixFlowNucleus`, and
`CollapseShuffle` identifies recognitions whose traces differ only by admissible
reordering.  The composition therefore picks out exactly those recognitions
whose stabilized form is invariant under (a) the global dynamics and closure,
and (b) the reordering of trace-progress steps.  These recognitions do not care
which admissible trace we used to reach them, so they define the shuffle
profile.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

{{ act_nav(page.meta) }}

layer: act
status: plan
---
# Leading

## Interpretation

*Entry* reveals two positions but remains agnostic about which leads.  Allowing the act *Entry* to witness the act *Recognition* selects the leading orientation.

The composition `(Entry Recognition)` displays the act *Entry* witnessing one copy of *Recognition*.  This observation singles out the first position, so we call the result *Lead Entry*.

# Trailing

## Interpretation

After identifying the leader, we must characterize the remaining position; *Entry* witnessing *Lead Entry* yields the trailing orientation.

In `(Entry LeadEntry)` the act *Entry* now watches *Lead Entry*, explicitly exposing the complementary position.  This composition therefore defines *Trail Entry*.

# Advancing Trace

## Interpretation

*Start Trace* shows how the trace begins but not how it proceeds once the trailing orientation completes.  Letting the trailing orientation witness *Entry* restarts the act, earning ***Advance Trace*** ([AdvanceTrace](advance-trace.md)).

The unresolved propagation after *Start Trace* is how the trailing side re-engages the structural pairing.  Having *Trail Entry* witness *Entry* provides that progression, so `(TrailEntry Entry)` defines *Advance Trace*.

# Starting Trace

## Interpretation

With leading and trailing orientations established, letting *Lead Entry* witness *Trail Entry* initiates the recorded trace.

The tree `(LeadEntry TrailEntry)` states that the act *Lead Entry* witnesses the act *Trail Entry*.  This first oriented pairing marks the beginning of the trace, hence the name *Start Trace*.

# Completing Trace

## Interpretation

*Continue Trace* alone does not record the full trace object.  Forcing *Start Trace* to witness *Advance Trace* binds the initial step with its progression, yielding ***Complete Trace*** ([CompleteTrace](complete-trace.md)).

Both components are available; composing them records the entire act of initiating and advancing, so `(StartTrace AdvanceTrace)` earns *Complete Trace*.

# Continuing Trace

## Interpretation

*Advance Trace* reconnects the trailing orientation to *Entry* but leaves undefined how a new leading orientation emerges.  *Entry* must therefore witness *Start Trace*, producing ***Continue Trace*** ([ContinueTrace](continue-trace.md)).

The only new symbol is *Start Trace*, already earned, so composing it with *Entry* is legitimate.  This confirms the recurring structure, so *Continue Trace* is forced.

# Capping Recognition

## Interpretation

Dually, we require the maximal recognition that witnesses every trace.  *Recognition* witnessing *Complete Trace* produces ***Cap Recognition*** ([CapRecognition](cap-recognition.md)).

The act uses existing recognitions and records the trivial recognition that all traces satisfy, so *Cap Recognition* is valid.

# Heading Trace

## Interpretation

*Complete Trace* captures the trace but leaves the immediate act unspecified.  Letting the leading orientation witness the whole trace extracts its head.

*Lead Entry* can now observe *Complete Trace*, isolating the first act present.  The tree uses only earned terms, so ***Head Trace*** ([HeadTrace](head-trace.md)) is valid.

# Tailing Trace

## Interpretation

Once the head is isolated, the remainder must also be named, so we let the act *Trail Entry* witness the act *Complete Trace* to obtain the tail.

The composition `(TrailEntry CompleteTrace)` shows the act *Trail Entry* witnessing *Complete Trace*.  This mirrors the head construction and therefore captures the remainder as *Tail Trace*.

# Decomposing Trace

`StartTrace`, `AdvanceTrace`, `ContinueTrace`, and `CompleteTrace` record how a
trace grows step by step, but nothing yet states the recursive *shape* of an
arbitrary completed trace.  The head–tail recognitions
([HeadTrace](head-trace.md), [TailTrace](tail-trace.md),
[TraceStep](trace-step.md), [ReheadTrace](rehead-trace.md),
[RetailTrace](retail-trace.md)) jointly witness the full record of
[CompleteTrace](complete-trace.md), so letting `HeadTrace` observe `TailTrace`
forces the decomposition of a trace into its left and right predecessors.

The composition `(HeadTrace TailTrace)` carries only previously earned data.
`HeadTrace` isolates the leading orientation of any completed trace while
`TailTrace` isolates the remainder, and the earlier recognitions ensure the tail
re-enters the trace (via `TraceStep`, `RenewTrace`, `ReheadTrace`,
`RetailTrace`) exactly as a shorter trace would.  Therefore
***DecomposeTrace*** witnesses the structural fact that every completed trace
splits into two smaller traces: the head’s continuation and the tail’s
continuation.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

{{ act_nav(page.meta) }}

# Stepping Trace

## Interpretation

Even with head and tail, we must show how removing the head re-engages the start, so the act *Tail Trace* witnesses the act *Start Trace* to produce the step.

The tree `(TailTrace StartTrace)` explicitly pairs the act *Tail Trace* with the act *Start Trace*.  This records how the remainder re-enters the beginning, which is precisely the role of *Trace Step*.

# Renewing Trace

## Interpretation

After stepping, the renewed orientation must contact the full record, so the act *Trace Step* witnesses the act *Complete Trace*.

In `(TraceStep CompleteTrace)` the act *Trace Step* observes *Complete Trace*, confirming that the renewed orientation spans the entire record.  This composition is therefore *Renew Trace*.

## Interpretation

Renewing alone does not isolate the next head, so the act *Renew Trace* must witness the act *Head Trace* to define the new head.

The composition `(RenewTrace HeadTrace)` shows the act *Renew Trace* watching *Head Trace*.  This isolates the new immediate act, so we name it *Rehead Trace*.

# Restepping Trace

## Interpretation

The renewed tail must rejoin the stepping process, so the act *Retail Trace* witnesses the act *Trace Step*.

In `(RetailTrace TraceStep)` the act *Retail Trace* observes *Trace Step*, recording the renewed tail re-entering the stepping act.  This is exactly what *Restep Trace* denotes.

tags:

---
# Shuffling Trace

Many trace acts commute: [ReheadTrace](rehead-trace.md) reorders against
[RetailTrace](retail-trace.md), [RestepTrace](restep-trace.md) reorders against
[TraceStep](trace-step.md), and each head/tail act commutes with the earlier
progressions exposed by [CompleteTrace](complete-trace.md).  No recognition yet
packages these commutations into a single principle, so we force the renewed
head to witness the stepping remainder: `(ReheadTrace RestepTrace)` earns
***ShuffleTrace***.

`ReheadTrace` and `RestepTrace` already demonstrate the two independent ways a
trace can progress: renewing the head or renewing the remainder.  Their
composition shows these progressions can be swapped without violating any
earned admissibility.  Thus `ShuffleTrace` records that the family of
trace-progressing acts forms a free shuffle algebra—any ordering built from
these steps is admissible, and admissibility is closed under permutation of
independent steps.


{{ act_info(page.meta) }}

CCE Application Binary Interface (CCE-ABI)

Specification – Version 0.x (normative)

Layer Declaration — CCE-ABI (Layer 4: Host Boundary)

This document occupies Layer 4 (CCE‑ABI) of the GFRTU stack:

  • Role: defines the lowest‑level, language‑agnostic binary interface for calling the CCE closure operator from host runtimes.
  • Depends on: the operational semantics of CCE (Layer 3, cce.md) and the canonical JSON encodings from CDS/RTL (Layer 2), but not directly on GFRTU mathematics.
  • Provides to higher layers: a stable FFI surface that preserves canonical bytes, hashes, and error codes while remaining agnostic to transport or RDF projection.
  • Must not do: redefine closure behavior, invariant catalogues, or evidence semantics; those remain normative in cce.md and are only transported here.

ABI implementers SHOULD treat this layer as a thin membrane that preserves the invariants and determinism established by CCE rather than a place to introduce new semantics or logging regimes.

CCE Remote Procedure Call (CCE-RPC)

Specification – Version 0.x (normative)

Layer Declaration — CCE-RPC (Layer 5: Network Boundary)

This document occupies Layer 5 (CCE‑RPC) of the GFRTU stack:

  • Role: defines a transport‑neutral JSON envelope for invoking CCE over networked or interprocess channels.
  • Depends on: CCE’s operational semantics (Layer 3, cce.md) and the ABI‑level expectations about canonical JSON and error codes (Layer 4, cce-abi.md). It assumes CDS/RTL encodings (Layer 2) as payloads.
  • Provides to higher layers/external systems: a replayable request/response transcript that preserves canonical bytes, hashes, and repair/evidence logs produced by CCE.
  • Must not do: introduce new closure behavior, determinism guarantees, or mathematical constraints beyond forwarding what CCE already defines.

Where this specification restates determinism, evidence ordering, or error mapping, it does so purely as a projection of Layer 3 obligations onto transport; CCE remains the single source of truth for closure semantics.

Cellular Closure Engine (CCE)

Specification – Version 0.x (normative)

Layer Declaration — CCE (Layer 3: Operational Closure)

This document occupies Layer 3 (CCE) of the GFRTU stack:

  • Role: defines the deterministic closure operator over CDS cells and RTL judgments, including normalization, invariant checking, repair strategies, and refinement of structural state.
  • Depends on: GFRTU mathematics (gfrtu.md) for the three fixed‑point principles, and on the structural encodings in CDS/RTL (Layer 2: cds.md, rtl.md).
  • Provides to higher layers: canonical closure behavior, error surface, and repair/evidence logs consumed by ABI (Layer 4), RPC (Layer 5), and RDF projection (Layer 6).
  • Must not do: introduce new wire formats, binary layouts, or network semantics beyond those delegated to cce-abi.md and cce-rpc.md.

Whenever this specification mentions canonical JSON hashes, repair logs, or evidence vectors, those duties are owned by CCE as the operational reflection of GFRTU closure; ABI/RPC layers are required only to preserve them transparently.

Cellular Data Serialization (CDS)

Specification – Version 0.x (Draft)

  1. Introduction

The Cellular Data Serialization (CDS) defines a complete, self‑contained, canonical, machine‑readable serialization of a single local universe (a cell) of a Generative Fibered Relational Trace Universe (GFRTU).

Mathematically, a cell is a sheaf topos of the form:

𝑅 𝑈

𝑆 ℎ ( 𝑈 , 𝐽 ∣ 𝑈 ) , R U ​

=Sh(U,J∣ U ​

),

where 𝑈 ⊆ 𝑇 U⊆T is a full subcategory of the global trace site and 𝐽 ∣ 𝑈 J∣ U ​

Cellular Data Storage (CDS‑Storage)

Specification – Version 0.x (normative)

Layer Declaration — CDS‑Storage (Layer 2s: Storage Substrate)

This document occupies Layer 2s (CDS‑Storage) of the GFRTU stack:

  • Role: defines a canonical, content‑addressed, immutable storage substrate for CDS cells and related artifacts.

  • Depends on: the CDS layer (cds.md) for the JSON shape of a cell, canonicalization rules, and hashing discipline.

  • Provides to higher layers: a stable mapping from cell hashes to canonical CDS files for CCE (Layer 3), ABI (Layer 4), RPC (Layer 5), RDF/PKM/CMS projections, and other tools.

Cellular Data Serialization (CDS)

Specification – Version 0.x (normative)

Layer Declaration — CDS (Layer 2: Structural Representation)

This document occupies Layer 2 (CDS) of the GFRTU stack:

  • Role: defines the canonical JSON shape of a cell and its structural state.
  • Depends on: the mathematical GFRTU layer (gfrtu.md) for meaning of sites, fibers, fixed layers, and cells.
  • Provides to higher layers: the byte‑level carriers that CCE (Layer 3), ABI (Layer 4), RPC (Layer 5), and RDF projection (Layer 6) consume.
  • Must not do: introduce new mathematical laws beyond gfrtu.md or redefine operational closure/repair behavior owned by cce.md.

Interpretive comments that mention sheafification, σ/Δ stabilization, or generative closure are explanatory pointers back to gfrtu.md and cce.md, not additional constraints. When in doubt, treat CDS as a structural contract and defer mathematical semantics to Layer 1 and operational semantics to Layer 3.

Phase 1 – Workspace + Determinism Contract

0. Phase objective

At the end of Phase 1 you have:

  • A Rust workspace whose crate boundaries mirror the spec documents (CDS, RTL, CCE, ABI, RPC, RDF).
  • A written determinism and normativity contract that all subsequent code must obey.
  • Stub APIs and types that establish the main data‑flow, but do not yet implement semantics.
  • No behavior needs to be correct yet, but the structure and constraints do.

You create a Cargo workspace (e.g. cce-rs/) with at least these crates:

Node Addressing (node-addressing.md)

  1. Purpose and Scope

This document specifies how to identify nodes and address artifacts (cells, judgments, closure runs, evidence) that a node serves.

It ties together:

Content identifiers from CDS and CDS‑Storage (cell‑ids, judgment hashes).

cds

Local storage paths inside a CDS‑Storage root.

cds

Network URIs for HTTP‑style access to those same artifacts, reusing the URI patterns recommended by the RDF profile where possible.

Node Runtime and Cycles (Node-Cycle)

Specification – Version 0.x (normative)
Document: node-cycle.md

  • Role: defines the lifecycle, health model, and request/response cycles of a node: a long‑running process that owns a CDS‑Storage root, embeds a CCE instance, and optionally exposes CCE‑RPC.
  • Depends on:
    • GFRTU mathematics (gfrtu.md) for the meaning of cells and closure. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}
    • CDS (cds.md) for the JSON structure and hashing discipline of cells. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}
    • CDS‑Storage (cds-storage.md) for on‑disk layout and store operations. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
    • RTL (rtl.md) for canonical judgments and normalization. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}
    • CCE (cce.md) for closure semantics, repair logs, and error surface. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}
    • CCE‑ABI (cce-abi.md) for host‑level FFI contracts. :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}
    • CCE‑RPC (cce-rpc.md) for network envelopes and evidence vectors. :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}
    • RDF Profile (rdf-profile.md) for URI patterns and external projection (optional). :contentReference[oaicite:7]{index=7}
  • Provides to higher layers: a deterministic runtime target for agents, CLIs, and services that need to:
    • store and retrieve cells,
    • refine cells via CCE, and
    • invoke CCE over RPC with reproducible transcripts.

This document must not redefine CDS, RTL, CCE, ABI, or RPC semantics. Where this specification speaks about canonical bytes, closure, or evidence, it is restating obligations from those documents, not introducing new ones.

1. Purpose and Scope

A node is the concrete runtime unit that:

owns a CDS‑Storage root for canonical GFRTU cells;

cds-storage

runs the Cellular Closure Engine (CCE) over those cells via a local ABI;

exposes closure and storage over a network boundary using CCE‑RPC envelopes;

cce-rpc

optionally projects canonical artifacts into RDF/JSON‑LD.

rdf-profile

Nodes are purely operational: they do not add mathematics beyond GFRTU, nor new structural formats beyond CDS/RTL/CCE.

This spec defines:

RDF Profile for GFRTU Cells and Closure Evidence

Specification – Version 0.x (normative)

Layer Declaration — RDF Profile (Layer 6: External Semantic Projection)

This document occupies Layer 6 (RDF projection) of the GFRTU stack:

  • Role: defines how canonical JSON artifacts from CDS/RTL/CCE/ABI/RPC are projected into RDF/JSON‑LD for identification, provenance, and federation.
  • Depends on: structural encodings (Layer 2), operational semantics and evidence logs (Layer 3), ABI/RPC envelopes (Layers 4–5), and indirectly on GFRTU mathematics via those lower layers.
  • Provides to external ecosystems: URIs, vocabularies, JSON‑LD contexts, and optional SHACL shapes that describe but never alter canonical artifacts.
  • Must not do: introduce new closure rules, canonicalization logic, or mathematical constraints; all such semantics remain in gfrtu.md, cds.md, rtl.md, and cce*.md.

Any determinism or provenance requirements mentioned here are reflections of lower‑layer obligations and MUST NOT be treated as an alternative or competing source of semantics.

Recognition Term Language (RTL)

Specification – Version 0.x (normative)

Layer Declaration — RTL (Layer 2: Structural Judgments)

This document occupies Layer 2 (RTL) of the GFRTU stack:

  • Role: defines the JSON encoding of judgments Γ⊢M:A\Gamma \vdash M : A and the internal term calculus for recognition fibers over a GFRTU cell.
  • Depends on: the GFRTU mathematics (gfrtu.md) for the interpretation of judgments as arrows in the sheaf topos, and on CDS (cds.md) for the structural context in which judgments live.
  • Provides to higher layers: canonical judgment payloads and normalization rules that CCE (Layer 3) applies when deciding admissibility.
  • Must not do: redefine closure/repair processes, logging, or transport behavior specified in cce.md, cce-abi.md, or cce-rpc.md.

Determinism and fuel accounting are specified here only as properties of RTL normalization; the use of fuel, repair logs, and evidence vectors is owned by CCE (Layer 3) and its ABI/RPC surfaces (Layers 4–5).

Relational Computer Architecture Specification

0. Purpose and Scope

This document defines the Relational Computer as a complete computational architecture derived from an underlying mathematical derivation. It contains everything required for an implementation to realize the architecture without reference to any other materials.

Goals:

Specify the machine’s primitive runtime structures.

Define the Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) of the machine.

Describe profiles as emergent semantic spaces with internal syntax, typing, and judgment systems.

Define the observation pipeline that governs how external information is interpreted and stabilized.

Provide explicit mapping tables between primitive architectural components and their roles needed for implementation.

Non-goals:

Generative Sheaf Universe and Acts of Relationality: Mathematical Specification

0. Preface

This document presents a complete, standalone mathematical specification of the structure called the Generative Sheaf Universe. It is intended for a reader fluent in category theory, sheaf theory, modal algebra, and intuitionistic logic. No knowledge of any external derivation, computer architecture, or applied semantics is assumed.

Historically this structure was called the Hypertensor Topos. In this revision we adopt the following notational changes, which will be used uniformly throughout:

Hypertensor Topos Specification 0. Preface

This document presents a complete, standalone mathematical specification of the structure we call the Hypertensor Topos. It is intended for a mathematically literate reader fluent in the language of category theory, sheaf theory, modal algebra, and intuitionistic logic. No knowledge of any external derivation, computer architecture, or applied semantics is assumed or required.

The Hypertensor Topos is defined entirely by a minimal collection of primitive data: a category of finite traces, fiberwise Heyting–modal algebras, functorial reindexing, a Grothendieck topology regulating gluing, and a small distinguished set of structural operations. From these ingredients alone, an internally rich, geometrically textured, and logically expressive universe emerges. It exhibits:

On Relationality

These documents are an exploration of relationality: the claim that things only exist through acts of relating.

There’s a program of formal math, and more stuff soon.

On Relational Calculus (Unified Draft)

This document rebuilds the relational calculus from a single act of Reflex and carefully records every recognition earned thereafter. Each definition follows the same format:

  1. Story – the tension that forces the recognition.
  2. Trace – the earned act written in the relational tree language.
  3. Proof Sketch – a brief justification that the recognition necessarily follows.

Every trace only uses symbols that have already been earned earlier in the document.

Relationality Research Program Repository

This repository is the home of the Relationality Research Program. It implements a Stewardable Semiotic Concept Universe (SSCU) backed by a flatfile datastore under content/ and operative code under src/. The operative code is the current Semiotic Publishing Service within the broader Semiotic Software Engine, turning Markdown concept artifacts into an evaluated SSCU instance and rendering HTML projections without writing intermediate trees inside the repo.

Repository layout

  • content/: flatfile datastore containing the universe’s Markdown concepts and metadata that instantiate the Stewardable Semiotic Concept Universe.
  • src/: operative code for the Semiotic Publishing Service (part of the Semiotic Software Engine) that evaluates the SSCU snapshot and emits projections.
  • universe.yaml and presets (e.g., content-site.universe.yaml, emsenn-net*.universe.yaml): publishing configurations that control which content subsets and HTML options are applied.

Conceptual mapping

  1. Markdown → Const/AnnTerm: Each Markdown file contains YAML frontmatter with id, title, optional tags, ann (annotation terms), and optional same_as declarations for ≈ transport.
  2. Ann_X(k): Annotation terms are parsed into the v0 AnnTerm language (AtomTerm, KeyValTerm, RefTerm). Unparseable terms become failure-marked.
  3. T_{k,X}: Interpretations ⟦t⟧ generate the fragment seeds. Ref terms are transported through the ≈ groupoid.
  4. F_{k,X}: The fragment contains generators; if any generator is ⊥_fail the fragment collapses to {⊥_fail}.
  5. Sem_X(k) = S(F_{k,X}): v0 applies the conservative closure S (identity with trace) and records provenance.
  6. HTML projection: The evaluated instance feeds projections (site:static, single:html). Failures and unresolved references surface in diagnostics. Concept pages also surface the interpreted generator set T_{k,X} and fragment elements F_{k,X} so readers can audit how annotations denoted into the partial structure.

Modes: strict vs permissive

  • Permissive (default): unresolved references are rendered as broken links/diagnostics but do not stop the build.
  • Strict: unresolved references trigger a build error.

HTML sanitization is enabled by default; set html.sanitize: false in universe.yaml to allow raw HTML from Markdown.

A Relational Generative Foundation for Computation and Geometry

A Unified Theory of Acts, Traces, Closure, and Aperiodic Operator Graphs

1. Introduction

This paper develops a unified mathematical and computational framework whose foundations emerge from a single generative principle: reflexive witnessing. From this primitive, the system expands by forced necessity into a hierarchy of structures that encompass:

  • a reflexive algebra of “Acts,”
  • a trace calculus capturing temporal and causal structure,
  • a closure-flow–shuffle operator triad with fixed-point invariants,
  • a lattice-theoretic internal logic,
  • a normalizing syntax and semantics,
  • a tower of profiles and nuclei functioning as local toposes,
  • a spectral geometry built from deviation operators, harmonic modes, and wave operators,
  • and a fully realized computational machine — the Aperiodic Operator Graph (AOG) — in which all computation is evaluation of canonical morphisms.

Unlike traditional foundations, where logic, syntax, computation, and geometry are axiomatically imposed, this system derives all structural components through generativity constraints. Each named Act arises only when its existence is required by a compositional inconsistency or an unresolvable ambiguity in prior Acts. The result is not a “designed system” but a forced closure of a free reflexive algebra — a universe where computation, semantics, geometry, and physics are not distinct layers but mutually entailed consequences of the same primitive.

TODO

This roadmap translates the relational mathematics (acts, profiles, hypertensors) into a software stack. Each phase builds on earlier ones; finish phases in order so the runtime remains coherent.

Phase 0 — Mathematical + Content Grounding

  • Audit the acts, strata, and texts to produce a canonical taxonomy of recognitions, contexts, and morphisms suitable for storage.
  • Decide how much metadata a “context node” must carry (stratum, reflex depth, tags, parent references, allowed morphisms).
  • Extract rewrite rules, flow/nucleus relationships, and hypertensor definitions from the docs into structured specification.

Phase 1 — Core Data Layer

  • Stand up XTDB locally.
  • Define schemas for:
    • Context nodes (IDs, strata, reflex depth, metadata blobs, pointers to hypertensor payload locations).
    • Directed morphisms (refinement/abstraction/restriction/extension edges with type metadata, provenance, effective dates).
    • Local neighborhood indexes that support lookup-by-stratum and path queries for rewrite planning.
  • Provision S3/MinIO buckets and decide on sparse binary formats for hypertensor payloads (e.g., compressed ndarray shards). Record URIs inside XTDB nodes.

Phase 2 — Runtime Rewrite Engine

  • Implement the normalization / rewrite core in Rust:
    • Encode acts as rewrite rules operating over context neighborhoods.
    • Ensure Flow/Nucleus interactions are first-class operations.
  • Expose the engine through WASM modules so Python, MCP services, or CLI tools can invoke rewrites deterministically.
  • Define the EDN for rule declarations, type metadata, and effect annotations; build parsers/validators.
  • Integrate with Tokio so localized patches pulled from XTDB execute concurrently while preserving causal ordering.

Phase 3 — Hypertensor Layer

  • Implement quasicrystalline hypertensor objects in Rust (ndarray backend first; leave CUDA/XLA hooks for later).
  • Define serialization for lattice coordinates + recognition payloads so fibers can be stored/retrieved as sparse blobs.
  • Implement pushforward/pullback operations induced by morphisms, ensuring they respect Flow covariance and Nucleus stability.
  • Write adapters that let the rewrite engine fetch/update hypertensor slices via XTDB references + object storage.

Phase 4 — Distributed Context Agents

  • Package each context as a lightweight Rust service that:
    • Watches XTDB for relevant changes.
    • Maintains its local hypertensor slice.
    • Runs rewrite steps via the WASM-exposed engine.
  • Use NATS for regulator/feedback messaging between agents (per stratum and cross-stratum signals).
  • Deploy via Nomad or Kubernetes, using Consul/etcd for discovery and secrets. Ensure agents can scale horizontally and rehydrate from XTDB snapshots.

Phase 5 — Observability + Control Loops

  • Emit metrics (rewrites, flow steps, regulator signals, latency) via Prometheus exporters; craft Grafana dashboards per stratum and per agent.
  • Instrument rewrites and morphism traversals with OpenTelemetry spans; ship to Jaeger for trace analysis.
  • Implement at least one regulator loop (e.g., stability enforcement across strata) over NATS to demonstrate Ashby-compliant heterogeneous control.

Phase 6 — Minimal End-to-End Prototype

  • Populate XTDB with a small subset of contexts/morphisms derived from the docs.
  • Store one hypertensor blob and prove pushforward/pullback works through a morphism.
  • Run a single agent + regulator loop that performs rewrites on demand and emits telemetry.
  • Validate OAuth/MCP integration requirements (auth story) and document the API surface to be formalized in OpenAPI later.

Phase 7 — Expansion + Productization

  • Design the OpenAPI schema + MCP services that expose site objects, fibers, and rewrite operations.
  • Layer OAuth scopes over context strata and morphism types.
  • Gradually ingest the full act catalogue, automate hypertensor generation for new content, and onboard heterogeneous inputs (text, citations, media, MCP tools) by mapping them to contexts + fibers.

item.title: ActivityPub on the Relational Computer
item.description:
Idea: ActivityPub as Recognitions

This design note describes how to host an ActivityPub-compatible social server on the universal relational computer. The goal is to treat ActivityPub objects (Actors, Notes, Follows, Likes, etc.) as recognitions, so that:

  • intake, topology, measurement, and traceability apply to all social activity,
  • social graphs, posts, and interactions live in the same lattice as math, MUD, ecology, and personal notes.

1. Core mapping

We model ActivityPub concepts as recognitions:

item.title: Concept A
item.description: A references B.

item.title: Concept B
item.description: B references missing C.

item.title: Frontmatter Schema for Earned Docs
item.description:
Frontmatter Schema

Frontmatter must be parseable into a typed DAG. The same core keys apply to structures and operators; operators add input/output wiring and a transformation DAG.

Common keys (all non-act docs)

  • id (string, required): unique, dot-free symbol matching the filename.
  • title (string, required): human-readable title.
  • role (enum, required): structure or operator.
  • status (enum, required): plan | draft | stable | deprecated.
  • proof_refs (array of string, optional): ids of acts/structures/operators cited in the body under ## Proof.

Structures

  • emerges_from (string, optional): act id when the act directly induces the carrier.
  • fields (array, required): each entry:
    • name (string): field name.
    • type (string): id of another structure (typed edge).
    • description (string): what the field means in the topos.

Structure example

id: hypertensor-section
title: Hypertensor Section
role: structure
status: draft
emerges_from: sheaf
fields:
  - name: window
    type: hypertensor-window
    description: locality of the section.
  - name: coverage
    type: coverage-site
    description: admissible sites the section ranges over.
proof_refs:
  - sheaf

Operators

  • inputs (array, required): each entry:
    • id (string): structure id consumed (typed edge).
    • alias (string): argument name.
  • outputs (array, required): each entry:
    • id (string): structure id produced (typed edge; include failure carriers if any).
    • alias (string): output name.
  • transformation (array, required): nested-array DAG encoding the morphism in terms of earned operators. The first element is an operator id; subsequent elements are literals or references/aliases. All operator ids referenced must have their own operator docs.

Operator example

id: join-recognition
title: Join Recognition
role: operator
status: draft
inputs:
  - id: recognition
    alias: left
  - id: recognition
    alias: right
outputs:
  - id: recognition
    alias: join
transformation:
  - meet-recognition
  - left
  - right
proof_refs:
  - recognition
  - meet-recognition

Validation rules (DAG)

  • Every type in fields must match a structure id.
  • Every id in inputs/outputs must match a structure id.
  • Every operator id referenced in a transformation must match an operator doc.
  • No dangling ids; the combined set of structures/operators must form a typed DAG.

item.title: Generative Fibered Recognition Trace Universe
item.description:
0. Introduction

This paper develops the mathematical foundations of a structure we call a
Generative Fibered Relational Trace Universe (GFRTU).
A GFRTU is a universe of semantic objects built from minimal relational data: a trace site, a family of fibered recognitions, and the dynamics of stabilization and drift.
Everything else—sheaf semantics, internal logic, local universes (cells), stability spaces, and higher layers of structure—is generated from these minimal commitments by three interacting closure principles.

item.title: Hive Auto Ledger
item.description:
Hive Auto Ledger

Last updated from triage notes on 2025-08-26.

Overview

Hive Auto-Ledger (HAL) is a speculative platform designed to manage Hive blockchain wallets with a focus on transparency, usability, and automation. The app will provide tools to track wallet contents, manage transactions, and analyze market data, all while maintaining an accurate Beancount ledger. Future iterations will integrate advanced features such as trade bots, post analytics, CMS capabilities, and tokenomics.

item.title: Ideas for the Universal Relational Computer
item.description:
Ideas Index

This directory collects prospective design notes for future slices of the universal relational computer. Each file is intentionally a plan, not code and not a binding specification: it names recognitions, measurements, topology, and runbooks for a domain so that, when the core system is ready, we can lift the idea into real specs and implementations under docs/app/spec/ and docs/app/arch/.

Shared pattern

Nearly every idea should answer the same questions:

item.title: internal-theory
item.description:
internal-theory (tag)

The internal-theory tag marks concepts that are primarily inward-facing mathematical or theoretical infrastructure: background derivations, foundational notes, and specifications that support the rest of the semiotic universe.

Use this tag for technical “under the hood” theory you expect to be referenced by other concepts more than read on its own.

item.title: mathematical-universe
item.description:
mathematical-universe (tag)

The mathematical-universe tag marks concepts that define or analyze a mathematical universe in the formal sense: fixed-point structures, internal logics, or ambient categories in which other objects live.

Use this tag when a document is primarily about the construction or properties of such a universe itself (e.g. the Semiotic Universe, GFRTU, the Stewardable Semiotic Concept Universe), rather than merely using a universe as background.

item.title: practice
item.description:
practice (tag)

The practice tag marks concepts that are grounded in concrete workflows, protocols, examples, or lived experience.

Use this tag when a document is primarily about doing—procedures, habits, experiments, or case studies.

item.title: Raw HTML payload
item.description: This body includes a raw script tag that should be removed when sanitization is enabled:

item.title: Semantics, Syntax, and Semiotics
item.description:
Semantics, Syntax, and Semiotics

Three Lenses on Meaningful Structure

This note clarifies how syntax, semantics, and semiotics relate to each other, and how each one shows up in the Semiotic Publisher and the Semiotic Universe.

It is intentionally practical: the goal is to help you read and write vault content in a way that makes the most of the semiotic tooling.

1. Syntax: Shape and Well‑Formedness

Syntax is the study of formal shape.

item.title: Semiotic Markdown
item.description:
Semiotic Markdown Specification

This specification describes how Markdown files, as ingested by the semiotic publisher [@semiotic-publisher], are interpreted as concepts in the Semiotic Universe [@semiotic-universe] and its Stewardable Semiotic Concept Universe extension [@stewardable-semiotic-concept-universe], and how their structure is mapped into atoms, fragments, and publication views.

Normative statements in this document use “MUST”, “SHOULD”, and “MAY” in the conventional requirements-language sense.

1. File Model

  • Throughout this specification, the loader denotes the Markdown‑to‑concept stage of the semiotic publisher [@semiotic-publisher].

item.title: Semiotic Publishing Script
item.description:
Semiotic Publishing Script

This is the documentation for the “semiotic publishing script,” the thing I use to make my website and other documents.

It works by treating a Markdown file as a [Stewardable Semiotic Concept Universe](Stewardable Semiotic Concept Universe), or combining a bunch of them into one universe.

1. High‑Level Overview

At a high level, the publisher does the following:

  1. Scan a vault of Markdown files with YAML frontmatter.
  2. Parse concepts: each file becomes a Concept with metadata, body, links, and annotations.
  3. Interpret annotations into atoms (tags, folders, links, status, type, facts, temporal markers).
  4. Configure j‑relations: a graph of implications between atoms (folder→tag, synonyms, status/type→tag).
  5. Compute fragments and semantics by iterated closure under j and G (trace).
  6. Evaluate concepts with a simple quantitative score based on semantic features.
  7. Build indices (tag, folder, type, status) over the universe.
  8. Export one or more targets:
    • html: a static site with an index and concept pages,
    • json: a static JSON API,
    • rdf: an RDF graph of concepts and atoms,
    • openapi: a static OpenAPI description of the JSON API,
    • pandoc: Markdown suitable for Pandoc (single concept or combined “book”).

The pipeline is driven from the CLI entrypoint and configured via a YAML file (semiotic.config.yml) plus command‑line overrides.

item.title: semiotics
item.description:
semiotics (tag)

The semiotics tag marks concepts that directly discuss semiotic theory or methods, or that lean heavily on semiotic structure as a primary lens.

Use this tag when a document is about semiotics, not merely using semiotic ideas in the background.

item.title: Signs, Signals, and Systems
item.description:
Signs, Signals, and Systems

How Semiotic Structure Shows Up in Practice

This note connects the abstract language of signs with the practical language of signals and systems.

It is meant as a bridge between the formal semiotic documents (like [The Semiotic Universe](The Semiotic Universe)) and everyday work with tools like the Semiotic Publisher.

1. Signs vs. Signals

In everyday work, we talk about signals:

  • “We need better signals for concept quality.”
  • “This tag is a signal that a document is ready for review.”
  • “The number of incoming links is a good signal of centrality.”

In semiotics, we talk about signs:

item.title: static-site
item.description:
static‑site (tag)

The static-site tag marks concepts that discuss static site generation, publishing pipelines, deployment concerns, and related infrastructure.

Use this tag when a document is about how the semiotic universe is rendered and served as static artifacts.

item.title: Status: deprecated
item.description:
deprecated (status)

The deprecated status marks concepts kept only for history and provenance.

  • Content may be outdated, superseded, or inconsistent with the current universe.
  • New work should avoid depending on deprecated concepts.
  • Links from active documents should explain why this concept is still referenced.

item.title: Status: draft
item.description:
draft (status)

The draft status indicates a concept that is coherent enough to read and use, but still expected to change.

  • Content may be incomplete or uneven.
  • Definitions and interfaces may shift.
  • The concept is ready for feedback and iteration, not yet for long‑term reference.

item.title: Status: sketch
item.description:
sketch (status)

The sketch status marks very provisional concepts.

  • Expect gaps, inconsistencies, and rough structure.
  • Suitable for personal thinking or pair work, not for unaccompanied reading.
  • A sketch is a candidate to be refined into a draft once the main spine is stable.

item.title: Status: stable
item.description:
stable (status)

The stable status marks concepts that are suitable as public reference.

  • Content is coherent, reviewed, and unlikely to change dramatically.
  • Interfaces, terminology, and definitions should be trustworthy for reuse.
  • Future edits should preserve backwards compatibility or record breaking changes explicitly.

item.title: stewardship
item.description:
stewardship (tag)

The stewardship tag marks concepts that focus on caring for, revising, curating, or governing semiotic structures and knowledge artifacts over time.

Use this tag when a document is about responsibilities, roles, and actions that keep a semiotic universe healthy, legible, and useful.

item.title: systems
item.description:
systems (tag)

The systems tag marks concepts that treat collections of entities and relations as wholes: feedback loops, networks, infrastructures, ecologies, or architectures.

Use this tag when a document steps back from individual concepts to talk about system‑level behavior and structure.

item.title: The Semiotic Universe
item.description:
The Semiotic Universe

A Fixed-Point Construction in a Modal–Comonadic Heyting Setting

Abstract

We construct a semiotic universe: a structure in which

  • semantic objects form a complete Heyting algebra equipped with
    • a modal closure operator jj, and
    • a trace comonad GG that is compatible with the Heyting structure;
  • syntactic operators are generated by a typed λ-calculus;
  • a fusion mechanism interprets syntactic operators as semantic endomorphisms and enforces coherence between syntax and semantics.

The semantic ambient is an algebraic poset:

item.title: The Stewardable Semiotic Concept Universe
item.description:
The Stewardable Semiotic Concept Universe

A Modal–Comonadic Heyting Framework for Stewardable Semantic Concepts

with Annotation Calculus, Constant Identity, Failure Semantics, Provenance, Delta, Subtraction, Interface Algebra, Fibration, Sheaf Semantics, and Coherence Theorems

Author: emsenn
Date: December 2025

Abstract

We extend the Semiotic Universe—a modal–comonadic Heyting structure equipped with definable operators from a typed λ-calculus—into a full Stewardable Semiotic Concept Universe. This extended universe supports the creation, annotation, revision, comparison, restriction, and evaluation of semantic concepts while preserving all structural invariants of the underlying Heyting–modal–comonadic semantics.

item.title: theory
item.description:
theory (tag)

The theory tag marks concepts that introduce or refine theoretical frameworks—definitions, theorems, constructions, and systematic perspectives.

Use this tag when a document is building or extending a formal or conceptual apparatus, rather than documenting a tool or practice directly.

item.title: tooling
item.description:
tooling (tag)

The tooling tag marks concepts that focus on software, scripts, or interfaces that support working within the semiotic universe.

Use this tag when a document is primarily about tools (CLIs, servers, editors, integrations) rather than the underlying theory itself.

item.title: Type: doc
item.description:
doc (concept type)

The doc type marks concepts that are primarily expository documents: essays, notes, or reference texts that carry most of their content in Markdown body text.

These are the default, document‑shaped nodes in the semiotic universe.

item.title: Type: tag
item.description:
tag (concept type)

The tag type marks concepts that serve as documentation hubs for tag atoms.

Each tag‑typed concept with id: <value> is treated as the canonical explainer for the tag:<value> atom and is surfaced at the top of /tags/<value>.html alongside the semantic neighborhood of that tag.

item.title: What Is Semiotics?
item.description:
What Is Semiotics?

Studying Signs, Not Just Symbols

Semiotics is the study of signs and sense‑making.

It asks a deceptively simple question:

How do things (marks, sounds, gestures, documents, systems) come to mean something for someone, in some situation?

Instead of treating meaning as a black box, semiotics turns it into something we can inspect, model, and steward.

1. Signs, Interpreters, Contexts

Semiotics usually starts from three ingredients:

  • a sign — something that stands for something else,
  • an object — what the sign is about,
  • an interpreter — someone or something for whom the sign functions.

Classic frameworks differ in emphasis:
